### --- Page 0012 ---

PREFACE TO THE SECOND EDITION,

In the preparation of this Edition, I have found it necessary to add a large amount of new matter, the result not merely of the decisions during the fifteen years which have elapsed since the first Edition, but of various recent Statutes affecting the Law of Infants, such as the Conveyancing and Law of Property Act, 1881, the Married Women’s Property Act, 1882, the Settled Land Acts, 1882 to 1889, and the Guardianship of Infants Act, 1886.

It was impossible for Mr. Simpson to foresee the full effect of the Infants Relief Act, 1874, and even now there are few decisions upon it, but I have, to the best of my ability, stated the law as it now stands, without in all cases entering so fully into the older law as Mr. Simpson thought it desirable to do.

The general provisions of the Settled Estates Act, 1877, appeared to me not to be of sufficient import-

<br>

### --- Page 0013 ---

viii PREFACE TO THE SECOND EDITION,

ance to be fully stated in the Text, and, together with those of the Settled Land Acts, are relegated to an Appendix; but the special provisions with regard to infants are of course retained.

In the Table of Cases I have added the references to contemporary Reports.

I have to express my thanks to Mr. Thos. Douglas of Lincoln’s Inn, Barrister-at-Law, for his valuable assistance, and especially for his careful revision of the Index.

EDGAR J. ELGOOD.

Lincoln’s Inn,  
March, 1890,

<br>

### --- Page 0014 ---

PREFACE TO THE FIRST EDITION.

THE following work has been written to supply a want which is believed to exist in the Profession. When it is remembered that the status of infancy covers nearly a third of the average period of human life, it may seem strange that no book on the subject has appeared since the treatises of Mr. Chambers and Mr. Macpherson, both published in 1842. Since that time many changes have taken place in the law, and a mass of new cases has been decided. When the author's thoughts were first turned to the subject, his intention was scarcely more than to bring Mr. Chambers’ book down to date; he soon found, however, that it was impossible to work with satisfaction on another man’s foundation, and accordingly the whole subject has been worked out independently; he desires, however, to acknowledge his obligations to Mr. Chambers’ treatise. By chance the author became possessed of that gentleman’s own copy of his book, which, in view of a second edition being called for, was in many places covered with marginal notes; he has on one or two occasions cited from this source notes of unreported cases, which are referred to as MS. Note, Chambers’ Infants, &c.

In so extensive a field the author cannot hope to have escaped errors both of omission and commission, but labour has not been spared to attain as far as possible fullness and accuracy. To bring the subject within the limits of a

<br>

### --- Page 0015 ---

x PREFACE TO THE FIRST EDITION.

In this short treatise, it has been necessary to aim at conciseness; while, therefore, no authority has been knowingly omitted, a few sentences, or even less, often give the result of decisions founded on long trains of reasoning. On one point (the right of a father to the earnings of his children), on which but little is to be found in the English books, the American cases are referred to.

In conclusion the author has to acknowledge valuable assistance received from Mr. Cecil C. M. Dale, of Lincoln’s Inn, Barrister-at-Law, while the book was passing through the press.

ARCHIBALD H. SIMPSON.

19, OLD Square, Lincoln’s INN.  
October, 1875.

<br>

ADDENDA ET CORRIGENDA.

Upon the construction of s. 1 of the Infants Relief Act, 1874, see Duncan v. Dizcn, W. N. 1890, 56; 6 Times L. R. 222.

In Duncan v. Dizcn, it was held that a marriage contract in the Scotch form for the settlement of all the property of the intended wife, then an infant, was voidable only, and not void; but the question appears not to have been ripe for decision whether, after majority, she had ratified it in fact, or could ratify it in law.

The ratio decidendi of Pornwall v. Hawkins applies to contracts entered into since the commencement of the Infants Relief Act, 1874; Whittingham v. Murdy, L. T. 966.

Add: A person who, whilst an infant, had robbed his master, on coming of age acknowledged in writing that he owed the master a certain sum, which he charged upon a legacy due to him, and it was held that the liability to be sued for the tort was a good consideration for the charge, which was enforceable accordingly; Sceley v. Briggs, 60 L. T. 665.

Where any consent to the marriage of any person is required as a condition, whether precedent or subsequent, the Court will, if possible, treat the consent to be given as a matter of substance, rather than of form; Daley v. Desbouverie, 2 Atk. 261; Keeling v. Smith, W. N. 1890, 37.

For ‘result’ read “resulting.” See Wells, 43 C. D. 281.

<br>

### --- Page 0118 ---

THE

LAW OF INFANTS.

INTRODUCTION.

THE law of infants is part of the jus personarum, or law of Status. It deals with the capacities and incapacities, privileges and exemptions, of all persons who have not attained to full age, and are therefore looked on as requiring a larger measure of legal protection.

The time at which a person is considered to attain full Age of majority must necessarily be somewhat arbitrarily chosen. According to the Roman law full age was fixed at twenty-five, but a distinction was drawn between those who were under and above the age of puberty. Males under fourteen and females under twelve were called “pupils,” and were under the guardianship of a “tutor;” after the above-mentioned ages they were considered to have attained years of discretion, but not full capacity to manage their affairs, and were under the care of a “curator.” A similar distinction still prevails in the law of Scotland.

Among some of the Northern nations the age of majority seems originally to have been fixed at the commencement of puberty; the age of fourteen, from analogy perhaps with the civil law, being chosen as the period in question. A youth of fourteen has clearly not arrived at the full strength of manhood, and as the use of heavy armour became prevalent, the age of twenty became adopted as the time of majority in military.

<br>

### --- Page 0119 ---

In military tenures,

In inferior tenures,

INTRODUCTION.

tenures. In such calculation of time a certain latitude was allowed: thus a day was allowed after a year, as shown in the common measure of a year and a day; and to the above periods of fourteen and twenty a year was added to enable the heir to sue out his livery and enter on his lands. Thus the Grand Coustumier de Normandie speaks of the heir in chivalry as being within age till he had accomplished twenty years; “et pour ce quilz doibuent estres tenues en guide, tunt que les vingt ans soient acumpliz:” but it adds “on leur donne ung an pur luisuge de Normandie en quoy ilz peuvent faire en court clameur et rappeller les suisines de leurs ancesseurs par enqueste. A celui qui a acompliz vingt et un ans nest pus otroyée Venqueste de la suisine a son ancesseur, se Vancesseur ne mourut dedens Uan et le jour que la clameur a est faicte (a).”

The age of twenty-one thus became the period of full age in the military tenures, but the lord might, if he pleased, terminate the wardship earlier by knighting the heir (b), and at fifteen might have an aid pur faire fitz chivaler (c).

In the inferior tenures the older period continued: thus Glanvil, in the reign of Hen. II, says, “Si vero haves et filius sokemanni fuerit, etatem habere intelligitur tune quum quindecim annos complererit (d).” In socage tenure the additional year dropped as being unnecessary, for the wardship belonged to the relations, who were accountable for the profits; and at the present day guardianship in socage ceases when the heir is fourteen. In gavelkind the additional year is still retained, for the heir was required to appear in court and demand his inheritance, which the lord was obliged to deliver (e).

(a) C. 33.

(b) Mag. Char. c. 3; 2 Inst. 11 & 12.

(c) Co. Litt. 78 b.

(d) Lib. 7. c. 9; and see to the same effect Bracton, f. 86 b.

(e) Robinson, Gavelkind, b. 2, c. 3. See Gilb, Ten., by Watk., Introd., Note 1, whence the above outline is taken.

<br>

### --- Page 0120 ---

INTRODUCTION, 3

Gradually the age fixed on as the period of majority Twenty-one in the military tenures became the period in all cases, duly fixed for tenants in socage, bondmen and even females (f); and this has long since become settled as the termination of infancy in all cases (g).

As a general rule in matters of civil capacity the law of England, unlike the Roman law, recognizes no distinction between infants above and under the age of puberty. “The law of England draws the line at the age of twenty-one, and therefore all courts must look upon a child of nineteen as a child of five years old; and you are by the law concluded from saying he is more capable at one period of his age than another: and this not only for the sake of himself but for the sake of his heirs (k).” And in speaking of infants suing by prochein ami, Parke, B., says, “The law knows of no distinction between infants of tender and of mature years; and as no special authority to sue is requisite in the case of an infant just born, so none is requisite from an infant on the very eve of attaining his majority (7).”

An infant attains majority on the completion of his twenty-first year, but, except where it is necessary for the purposes of the decision to show which of two events first happened (7), the law recognizes no fractions of a day, and therefore the year is regarded as completed on the first instant of the day before the birthday. Thus an infant born on the 1st February attains his majority at the first instant of the 31st January (k).

(f) 2 Watk. Cop. 77.

(g) Litt. s. 104.

(h) Per Sol.-Gen. arg., Hearle v. Greenbank, 3 Atk. 703.

(i) Morgan v. Thorne, 7 M. & W. p. 408.

(j) See Campbell v. Strangetcays, 3 C. P. D. 105; Clarke v. Bradlaugh, 8 Q. B. D. 63.

(k) Herbert v. Turball, 1 Keb. 389; Sid. 162; S. C., Anon. 1 Salk. 44; and see Sir B. R. Howard's case, 2 Salk. 625, Ld. Ray. 480; and the Bishop Castle case, Rogers on Elections, 14th ed. 118, where a voter, who was born at 9 o'clock on the morning of the 11th, was held entitled to vote on the morning of the 10th.

<br>

### --- Page 0121 ---

4

Division of subject.

INTRODUCTION.

The law of infants falls naturally into two main divisions; the first dealing with the capacities and incapacities of infants considered by themselves, in their dealings with others; and the second treating of the rights and duties of guardian and ward, both in relation to each other and to third persons. The law of parent and child is strictly speaking only a particular case of guardian and ward, but as it is distinguished by various important incidents, it will be more convenient to consider it separately. We shall also relegate to a separate part the practice of the Courts in carrying out the remedies given to infants. The subject will accordingly be divided into four parts. Part I. treats of the capacities and incapacities of Infants; Part II. the Law of Parent and Child. Part III., the Law of Guardian and Ward. Part IV., Procedure, or the Practice of the Courts in actions and proceedings by or against Infants.

<br>

### --- Page 0122 ---

PART I.  
CAPACITIES AND INCAPACITIES OF INFANTS.

CHAPTER I.

INSTRUMENTS AND ACTS WHICH ARE VOID.

The acts of an infant fall under three heads, according as they are (1) void, (2) voidable at the election of the infant or those claiming under him, (3) binding on him as fully as if he had attained twenty-one.

Before the recent statute (a), the distinction between void and voidable acts was of more importance than is at present. But s.1 of that Act makes void many contracts by infants which were formerly voidable only. It is in the following words:

“All contracts, whether by specialty or by simple contract, henceforth entered into by infants for the repayment of money lent or to be lent, or for goods supplied or to be supplied (other than contracts for necessaries), and all accounts stated with infants, shall be absolutely void (b); provided always, that this enactment shall not invalidate any contract into which an infant may by any existing or future statute (c), or by the rules of common law or equity, enter, except such as now by law are voidable.”

(a) The Infants Relief Act, 1874, 37 & 38 Vict. c. 62.

(b) It is submitted that this does not alter the law (see post, p. 12), by making the contracts and accounts stated void as against the other party; but there is as yet no decision upon this point.

(c) Eg., under the Infants Settlement Act, 1855; by bond under the Customs Act, 39 & 40 Vict. c. 36, s. 165; by membership of a building society, 37 & 38 Vict. c. 42, s. 38; or of a friendly society, 38 & 39 Vict. c. 60, s. 15 (8); or of an industrial society, 39 & 40 Vict. c. 45, s. 11 (9); or of a trade union, 39 & 40 Vict. c. 22, s. 9.

<br>

### --- Page 0123 ---

G INSTRUMENTS

Distinction The distinction, however, between void and voidable between said acts still remains, and is a fundamental one; a void act is not binding at the time, and can never be ratified (a); while a voidable act is binding until set aside (c). An illustration of this distinction is afforded by the different effects of re-delivery respectively of a deed by an infant after attaining twenty-one, and by a married woman after coverture has ceased: in the first case (assuming the deed to be voidable only) the deed is not made good, for it must date from the first delivery, and the second delivery is bad; while in the second case the first delivery being a nullity, the deed is good from the date of the second delivery (f).

Word “void” To is important to bear this distinction in mind, loosely use. isasmuch as in the old authorities the word “void” is very loosely used, an act being declared void where the only point necessary to the decision was that it was not binding. Thus in some of the older authorities, eg., Comyn’s Digest (g), it is said that infants’ deeds are void, as the grant of a rentcharge; the authorities referred to being Perk. 13 and Thompson v. Leach (h); but in the same section Perkins says that the grantor “cannot against such a deed in pleading say that he did not grant by the deed, for the deed is not void but voidable;” while Thompson v. Leach was the case of a person 207 compos, so that the observations as to infants are obiter dicta. So in 1 Roll. Abr. 930, a lease by an infant is said to be void, for an infant may bring trespass against the lessee, but in the next

(a) See Hicks v. Morant, 3 Y. & J. 286.

(c) See Oakes v. Turquand, L. R. 3 H. L. C. p. 875.

(f) Perk. s. 154; 1 Shep. Touch. p. 60. The infant's re-delivery would, however, generally operate as a confirmation. It has been said that an infant lacks both judgment and capacity, a feme covert capacity only: Compton v. Collinson, 2 B. C. C. p. 387; Sockelt v. Wrey, 4 tb. p. 486; see also Burnaby v. Equitable Reversionary Interest Society, 28 C. D. p. 424.

(g) Enf. c. 2.

(h) 3 Mod, 310.

<br>

### --- Page 0124 ---

AND ACTS WHICH ARE VOID.

In sentence it is said the infant may also bring an action for use and occupation, which shows that the lease is voidable only. Brooke (i) states an infant’s deed to be void, but, under the title “Dum fuit infra aet.,” he says that a grant by an infant is voidable not void, for delivery of a deed cannot be void but voidable. On the other hand, it is settled that the plea to a void deed is “non est factum,” but an infant cannot give evidence of infancy under this plea, he must plead infancy specially (k); and the cases of Zouch v. Parsons (l), Allen v. Allen (m) are conclusive to show that all deeds by an infant are not void.

Two rules have been laid down as to whether the deed of an infant is void or voidable: the first is that in Perkins (n), “All such gifts, grants, deeds made by an infant as do not take effect by delivery of his hand are void; but all gifts, grants or deeds made by an infant by matter in deed or in writing, which take effect by delivery of his own hand, are voidable by himself, his heirs and those who have his estate.” The second rule is that the instrument is void or voidable according to the semblance of benefit to the infant upon the face of it (o).

It is clear that the two rules are different: the first would render void a parol lease for years by an infant, however beneficial, because it does not take effect by delivery of his hand; and the second would render void a lease made without rent to try title, because on the face of the deed there is no semblance of benefit. The authorities decide that both these acts are voidable only (p).

(i) Abr. Covert, and Inf. pl. 1 & 10.

(k) Whelpdale's case, 5 Co. Rep. 119; Yates v. Bowen, 2 Stra. 1104; and the authorities there referred to; 3 Burr. 1805.

(l) 8 Burr. 1794,

(m) 2 Dru. & War. 307.

(n) S. 12,

(o) See Zouch v. Parsons, 3 Burr. 1804.

(p) Post, pp. 25, 26.

<br>

### --- Page 0125 ---

INSTRUMENTS

In so far as the rule in Perkins applies to deeds, it was much discussed in Zouch v. Parsons, and it was in effect laid down that an infant’s deed, where the delivery of it answers to the livery of seisin and operates as the conveyance of an interest, is merely voidable; but where it does not take effect as an assurance by delivery of the hand, as in a power of attorney, it is then actually void. To this Lord Mansfield added the qualification that if a case should arise where it would be more beneficial to the infant that the deed should be considered void; as if he might incur a forfeiture, or be subject to damage unless it was deemed void; the reason of an infant’s privileges would in such a case warrant an exception from the rule. It was not necessary to discuss which of the two rules was applicable, as according to either the deed was voidable only. Zouch v. Parsons was disapproved of by Mr. Preston, but it was expressly followed by Lord St. Leonards in Allen v. Allen. In that case father tenant for life, and infant son tenant in tail in remainder of an estate pur autre vie, resettled the property. After attaining twenty-one, the son confirmed the settlement. The original settlement was held to be voidable only on both grounds, viz., the solemnity of the instrument and the benefit of the infant; Lord St. Leonards saying that Zouch v. Parsons had proceeded chiefly on the latter. Where the instrument has no solemnity equivalent to feoffment or delivery, its validity must depend on the application of the second rule. This rule, however, seems to be stated in terms somewhat too narrow; an instrument may on the face of it appear beneficial and be in reality the reverse, or it may appear disadvantageous and not actually be so, or it may be impossible to say on the face of the instrument whether it is to the advantage.

<br>

### --- Page 0126 ---

AND ACTS WHICH ARE VOID

of the infant or not. The qualification to the first rule introduced by Lord Mansfield, viz. that a deed might be held void, if it were more beneficial to the infant so to hold it, seems to cut at the root of the first rule, inasmuch as it shows that an instrument may be void however great the solemnity it carries with it, and thus evidence dehors the deed must be admissible, a result which it apparently was the whole object of the rule to avoid. Though what the author ventures to submit is suggested the true rule seems never to have been laid down in distinct terms, it may be gathered from the language of several cases. Thus Lord Raymond states the rule to be that where the contract may be for the benefit of the infant or may be to his prejudice, the law so far protects him as to give him an opportunity to consider it when he comes of age, and it is good or voidable at his election. Lord Hardwicke referring to this case says that where an agreement appears on the face of it to be prejudicial to an infant, it is void, but if for his advantage, then voidable only; while Eyre, C. J., lays it down that contracts of an infant are void, if the Court can pronounce them to be to his prejudice. It would seem, then, that in considering whether an instrument is void or voidable, the true test at common law, irrespective of statute, is that all instruments and acts of an infant are voidable only, except those which are necessarily to his prejudice, these last being void. But the statute-law is much more favourable to infants.

(0) 8 Burr. 1807,

(u) Holt v. Ward, 2 Stra, 937.

(x) Harvey v. Ashicy, 8 Atk. 610.

(y) Keene v. Boycott, 2 H. Bl. 511.

(z) The correctness of this result, which was arrived at after an examination of the English cases only, is confirmed by the fact that the rule is stated in almost identical terms by Story, J., after an examination of the English and American cases; Tucker v. Morland, 1 Amer. Lead. Cases, 280. But in Pollock on Contracts, 5th ed. 59, it is submitted that at common law they are all voidable only.

<br>

### --- Page 0127 ---

Instruments and acts by an infant which are void, Appointment of attorney or agent void.

INSTRUMENTS

Those instruments and acts of an infant being void at common law, at any rate as against the infant, which are necessarily to his prejudice, it follows that, if an infant enfeoffs his guardian, it is void; so, when an infant released a trustee who had handed over only part of the trust funds, the release was said to be void both at law and in equity.

In many cases an instrument or act is held to be a nullity, because it must from its own nature be regarded as prejudicial, either by the doctrine of the law or the enactment of a statute. Thus it is a general rule that an infant cannot appoint an attorney or an agent. Such an appointment is void: consequently any act by such a person is void also as against the infant. A feoffment made by an infant who dies without heirs prevents an escheat; but if the feoffment be made by an attorney for the infant, it is void, and the escheat takes place. A letter of attorney by an infant to deliver seisin is void, and any person who claims by virtue of it may be treated as a wrongdoer. So an infant cannot be guilty of a forcible entry or disseisin by barely commanding or assenting to one to his use, for such a command is void. A lease by an agent is void, and the infant may bring ejectment without any notice to quit. A warrant of attorney is void, even though in the case quoted there were circumstances of fraud on the part of the infant. A cognovit is

(b) See the remark of Lord Mansfield, 3 Burr. 1807, and of Eyre, C.J., in Keene v. Boycott, 2 H. Bl. 514, quoted ante, p. 9.

(c) Bac. Abr. Inf. I. 3; 1 Roll. Abr. 728,

(d) Overton v. Banister, 3 Ha, 503.

(e) Oliver v. Woodroffe, 4 M. & W. 650.

(f) Doe v. Roberts, 16 M. & W. 778.

(g) 1 Roll. Abr. 730.

### --- Page 0128 ---

AND ACTS WHICH ARE VOID. 11

void (x), or a submission to a reference by an attorney (o). A recovery was reversed because an infant was vouchee, and appeared by attorney (p) instead of by guardian (q).

On the other hand, there are old authorities to show that an infant may appoint an attorney to accept livery of seisin, for this is for his benefit (r); and in Westcott v. Cotton (s) it is said that an infant may not defend an action by attorney; but may bring one because it is for his benefit. The fact that such an appointment can in any case be good shows the true reason of the converse rule, viz, that it is not generally for an infant’s benefit to have an attorney, and so the appointment of one is void as being prejudicial on the face of it: if the reason were that the deed does not take effect as an assurance by delivery of the hand (t), no appointment of an attorney could ever be more than a nullity: and with this agrees Bacon Abr. (w): “If the infant had made a letter of attorney to deliver seisin, he might have an assize, &c., because the letter of attorney, like all other acts of agreement made by an infant to his prejudice must be void; and therefore whoever claims under it, or by virtue of its authority, must be a wrong doer.”

We have seen (ante, p. 5), that many contracts by infants, whether by specialty or by simple contract, are now absolutely void by statute; and under s. 2 of the Infants Relief Act (x) those which are not void, cannot be effectually ratified. Contracts for necessaries, as to which see post, p. 87, are excepted (y).

### --- Page 0129 ---

12 INSTRUMENTS

Covenants. Before the Act of 1874, an infant could not bind himself by covenant (z); but his covenant was voidable only, not void, unless it was necessarily to his prejudice (a). In many cases the covenant will now be void.

Covenant bound. If an infant and adult bind themselves by joint and several covenants, the adult will be bound, though the infant is not (b). If an infant and an adult enter into mutual covenants, e.g., if the infant covenant to serve, and the adult to teach him, the infant may bring an action for breach of the covenant, though the other party could not (c). An infant may bind himself apprentice (d); but even in that case he cannot be sued upon his covenant to serve (e), except under the custom of London (f).

Covenants in marine. In all cases not within the Infants Settlement Act, 1855 (g), where an infant has covenanted to settle property by deed executed since the commencement of the Infants Relief Act, 1874, the covenant will, it is conceived, be incapable of being effectually ratified (h). But, under the equitable doctrine of election (i), the infant will seldom be entitled to any benefit under the

Whether infants’ bonds are voidable or void. But, except possibly when given for necessaries, the Act of 1874 makes them either void, or incapable of being sued on, though ratified after majority. As to bonds given for necessaries, see post, p. 93. If a man by will direct payment of a bond given during infancy, the executors cannot, it is presumed, refuse payment; Hampson v. Lady Sydenham, Nels, Ch. R. 55. It seems that by American law, a bond by an infant is voidable only; 1 Amer, Lead, Cas, 311.

(z) Johnson v. Boyfield, 1 Ves. jun. 314; Whittingham v. Hill, Cro. Jac. 494.

(a) Keene v. Boycott, 2 H. Bl. 511.

(b) Haw v. Ogle, 4 Taun, 10; Gillow v. Lillic, 1 Bing. N. C. 695, qu., however, if the covenant be joint only, Gillow v. Lillie.

AND ACTS WHICH ARE VOID. 13

settlement, without compensating the persons injured by his or her refusal to perform the covenant. For a statement of the law in cases not within the Act of 1874, see post, p. 29, 35.

An infant cannot make a will (j). Will.

Previously to the recent statute an account stated was voidable only, and might have been ratified after twenty-one by the infant (k); but it is now absolutely void, at all events against the infant (l).

An infant is not bound by a proviso in a deed to which he is not a party, though he take benefits under the deed. Thus where an infant was articled to a party solicitor, and in the articles, to which the infant was not a party, there was a proviso that he should not practise within a certain district, and the father covenanted that on attaining twenty-one the son should execute a bond to ensure fulfilment of the proviso, the infant served under the articles for three years after attaining twenty-one, but was never called on to execute the bond; the Court refused to restrain him from practising within the district (m). But, before the Act of 1874, an injunction was granted against a person who, while an infant, had himself entered into a somewhat similar contract (n); and an agreement by an infant to serve a dairyman at £1 a week, with stipulations that he would not serve for his own benefit any of the master's customers during the employment, or for two years afterwards, and for two weeks’ notice to leave on either side, was enforced against the infant, as being a beneficial contract, and not within the Act (o).

(j) 1 Vict. c. 26, s. 7. Under the old law he could make a will of personalty, though the exact age at which he might make it was doubtful; Co. Litt. 89 b. note 6.  
(k) Williams v. Moor, 11 M. & W. 256.  
(l) See, ante, p. 5 (o).  
(m) Capes v. Hutton, 2 Russ. 357.  
(n) Cornwall v. Hawkins, 41 L. J. Ch. 435.  
(o) Fellows v. Wood, 59 L. T. 513; but here the infant probably attained twenty-one before his breach of contract, as suggested in Francesco v. Barnum, 43 C. D. 165. See also post, pp. 94 (v), 96 (l).

<br>

### --- Page 0131 ---

Election.

Merger.

Order and disposition clause in bankruptcy.

INSTRUMENTS AND ACTS WHICH ARE VOID.

Where conversion of property is directed, an infant, though absolutely entitled, cannot elect to take it in its unconverted state (p); and on the same principle a merger of a charge in the inheritance will not take place, if it be to the disadvantage of the infant (q).

As an infant cannot “consent,” his goods do not pass to the trustee in bankruptcy by being left in the order and disposition of a bankrupt trader (r). As a cestui que trust absolutely entitled is the “true owner” within the meaning of the Bankruptcy Acts (s), if he be an infant, it is conceived that he could not be prejudiced by the bare trustee allowing his goods to remain in the order and disposition of a bankrupt trader (t). If, however, the trustee be not merely a bare trustee, but have powers of dealing with the property absolutely, it seems he would be considered as the “true owner” within the meaning of the Acts, although the cestui-que trust were an infant (u).

(p) See post, p. 84.

(q) Forbes v. Moffatt, 18 Ves. 384; Alsop v. Bell, 24 Beav. 451.

(r) Viner v. Cadell, 3 Esp. 88.

(s) Exp. Burbidge, 1 Deac. 131.

(t) See Lewin, 8th ed., 245.

(u) Exp. Dale, Buck. 365; and see Hensicy v. Wills, W. N. 1867, 172.

<br>

### --- Page 0132 ---

CHAPTER II.

INSTRUMENTS AND ACTS WHICH ARE VOIDABLE.

Section 1.—Matters of Record.

A JUDGMENT in law is as binding on an infant as on an adult, though he may get it set aside by showing that he was an infant; and in proceedings on judgments, in bankruptcy or against the property of infants, the Courts will, if necessary, inquire into the consideration for a judgment debt, and if it has been taken away by the Act of 1874 (see ante, p. 5, post, p. 19), they will disregard the judgment. A judgment obtained in an action by the next friend is binding on the infant, though he has never consented to the action being brought. The Court is not bound to set aside a judgment made against an infant, if the infant has no claim to the indulgence. If the judgment is set aside, the Court will not, except in special circumstances, restrain an infant from pleading infancy.

A judgment binds the inheritance of an infant, and an elegit may be sued out upon it.

A judgment entered up on a warrant of attorney will be vacated if entered up against an infant and bind the inheritance.

(a) Henry v. Archibald, Ir. Rep. 5 Eq. 559.

(d) Exp. Ribble, 10 Ch. 373.

(c) Re Onslow's Trusts, 20 Eq. 677.

(d) Morgan v. Thorne, 7 M. & W. 400; post, ch. xxxi.

(c) Wright v. Hunter, 11 J. K. B., O. S., 248; see post, p. 491 (n).

(f) Delafield v. Tanner, 5 Taun, 856.

(9) Re Howarth, 8 Ch. 418.

(k) Re South, 9 Ch. 369.

adults on a joint warrant, it will be vacated as to the infants, and left to stand against the others (k). Recognizances. Matters of record, as statutes merchant, and of the staple, and recognizances acknowledged by an infant, can only be avoided during minority (J). The Court on one occasion refused to discharge a recognizance entered into by an infant to appear and prosecute, which had become forfeited (m). Judgment of The jurisdiction and procedure of the Chancery Courts of Equity. Division of the Court in actions and proceedings by and against infants require separate treatment, and are fully discussed in chapters xxix., xxx., and xxxi., post. Bankruptcy. Under the old law, a commission of bankruptcy against an infant would be superseded on his own petition (7), or on that of a third person (o). Such a commission was void at law (p). But the Courts of Equity refused to interfere where the infant had been guilty of fraud (q); and under similar circumstances the Court of Bankruptcy refused to annul the fiat (7). A debt fraudulently contracted by an infant, who is made bankrupt after attaining his majority, may be proved against his estate (s). Formerly, where an infant was in partnership with adults, as a commission including the infant could not be supported,

18 INSTRUMENTS

that males or females be of full age, this shall be intended of the age of twenty-one years; for if before such age any deed or feoffment, grant, release, confirmation, obligation, or other writing, be made by any of them, all serve for nothing, and may be avoided.” “There is in law a total absolute disability in an infant, that by no manner of conveyance can he dispose of his inheritance” (b).

Feoffment. So a feoffment by an infant is voidable (c), except as to gavelkind land, the customs relating to which may be seen in 2 Dav. Conv. Pt. I., 4th ed., 244.

Declaration of use or trust: Though an infant cannot declare such a use as the Statute will execute, yet in conveyances which operate by transmutation of possession, as fine and recovery, or feoffment, he may declare the uses, and such declaration is voidable only (d); and as a grant is now in this respect on the same footing as a feoffment, an infant may graft a trust upon the legal estate, though, of course, such declaration would be voidable by him (e).

Bargain and sale and surrenders are, in fact, nothing more than declarations of uses: the bargainor or covenantor was deemed by the Court of Chancery to be seised to the use of the bargainee or covenantee, and this use was by the Statute of Uses clothed with the legal estate. For the purpose of raising a use some consideration was required; in the former case, money or other valuable consideration, and in the latter, blood or marriage. An infant cannot transfer the legal estate by either of these deeds, for his declaration of use is not one which the Statute of Uses would execute. Thus, a deed of bargain and sale by an infant is of no effect to raise a use (f); and if he covenants to stand seised to a use in consideration of blood or marriage, the use is void (g); but it would seem that

(b) Per Lord Hardwicke, Hearle v. Greenbank, 3 Atk. p. 712.
(c) Whittingham’s case, 8 Co. Rep. 43; Co. Litt. 337 b.
(d) Bac. Uses, L. T. 355; Gilb. Uses, 239.
(e) Lewin, 8th ed., 25.
(f) Gilb. Uses, 239; 2 Inst. 673.
(g) Bac. Uses, L. T. 355.

AND ACTS WHICH ARE VOIDABLE, 19

the deed of bargain and sale or covenant to stand seised stands upon the ordinary footing of an infant’s deed, and is voidable only, unless necessarily to his prejudice. Thus, if there be no consideration for a bargain and sale, though a consideration is expressed, such a deed is void (k): but in general his deed of bargain and sale enrolled is voidable (i): so before the Infants Relief Act, 1874, it would probably have been held that his covenant to stand seised to uses would become binding on him by confirmation after attaining twenty-one, and that an action would then lie against him on the covenant, as in the case of a covenant to stand seised to the use of a stranger, where also no use or estate arises (l). But under s. 2 of that Act no action could now be brought upon such a confirmation. This section is in the following words:—

“No action (1) shall be brought whereby to charge any person upon any promise made after full age to pay any debt contracted during infancy, or upon any ratification made after full age of any promise or contract (m) made during infancy, whether there shall or shall not be any new consideration for such promise or ratification after full age (n).”

"When it is said that such a use declared by an infant is void, probably all that is meant is, that it is not such

(h) Ibid.

(i) 2 Inst. 673,

(k) 2 Shep. Touch. 511.

(l) Including suit or other proceeding; Re Ouslow's Trusts, 20 Ey. 877; and probably “set-off,” see Rawley v. Rawley, 1 Q. B. D. 460 (a case on Lord Tenterden's Act, s. 5).

(m) These words are general, and include a promise to marry: Coxhead, Mullis, 3 B. D. 439; Ditcham v. Worrall, 5 ib. 410. The Act applies to ratifications made after its passing of contracts made before that time: Ex parte Kihlle, 10 Ch. 873.

(n) This section, as to which see post, ch. iii, s. 1., impliedly repealed s. 26 of 9 Geo. 4, c. 14 (Lord Tenterden’s Act), whereby it was enacted that the infant should not be charged except upon a written ratification. The Act was expressly repealed by the Statute Law Revision Act, 1875 (38 & 39 Vict. c. 66).

### --- Page 0137 ---

20

Lease and release.

INSTRUMENTS

a use as the Statute would execute during infancy: the precise effect of a confirmation by an infant after twenty-one, of a bargain and sale made during infancy, seems never to have been decided. It would probably be held that the use as well as the deed is made good from the beginning, and that the bargainee has as good an estate as if the bargain and sale had been made by an adult (o).

Prior to the Statute of Uses, if a man made a lease for years and the lessee entered, he might take a release of the reversion from the lessor, and thus become owner in fee without the necessity of livery of seisin; but for this purpose it was necessary that the lessee should actually enter, for if he had not actual possession, but only a right to it by the lease, the release was void (p). After the Statute of Uses the lease was, in fact, a bargain and sale for a year, and this raised a use for the lessee which the Statute executed, so that the land was actually vested in him without entry, and he could then take a release of the reversion (q). As the validity of the release depends upon the fact of the land being actually vested in the lessee, either by entry or by force of the Statute, if the execution by an infant of the deed of bargain and sale for a year is of no effect in raising a use, and the lessee do not enter, the release founded on it must necessarily be void. The validity of a lease and release by an infant was much discussed in the case of Zouch v. Parsons (r). A man seised in fee mortgaged land; the mortgagee died, leaving an infant heir, who was also one of his executors. The mortgage money was paid off by the lessors of the plaintiff, who at the same time made further advances.

(o) See Zouch v. Parsons, 3 Burr. 1794; and in America, Jackson v. Burchin, 14 John, 126; Tucker v. Moreland, 1 Amer. Lead. Cas. 280. 

(p) Litt. s. 459; Co. Litt. 270 a.

(q) Harg. Co. Litt. n. 227.

(r) 8 Burr. 1794.

<br>

### --- Page 0138 ---

AND ACTS WHICH ARE VOIDABLE.

to the mortgagor, and by deeds of lease and release, to which the infant and the executors were parties, the mortgaged land was conveyed to the transferees. The infant entered for the purpose of avoiding his conveyance, and the lessee of the transferees brought an action of ejectment to recover possession. At the date of the action the infant was still under twenty-one, so that there was no question of confirmation. It was held by Lord Mansfield that, supposing the conveyance to be voidable, the entry by the infant while still under age had not avoided it; that the plaintiff's title was good, 1st, because a deed by an infant was voidable only, and binding till it was set aside; and 2ndly, as the infant only did what the Court of Chancery would have compelled him to do, the conveyance was actually binding on him. The mode in which a lease and release operates, viz., by raising a use, was not touched upon; but it follows from the decision, either that the transferees had perfected the bargain and sale for a year by entry, which is nowhere stated, or that a use declared by an infant for the purpose of effecting transmutation of possession is not necessarily one which the Statute will not execute; but that if the Court see it is for the infant’s benefit, or it would seem from analogous reasoning, if the infant confirms the deed declaring the use after twenty-one, such a use is good from the beginning. Zouch v. Parsons was disapproved of by Mr. Preston (s), but has the sanction of Lord St. Leonards (t) and of Lord Eldon (u), and is now regarded as having been well decided (2).

If an infant exchange lands, and after full age occupy the lands taken in exchange, the exchange is become perfect, for the exchange at the first was not void (because it amounted to a livery, and also in respect

(t) Allen v. Allen, 2 Dru. & War. 307.

(u) Anon. v. Handcock, 17 Ves. 383.

(2) Per Pearson, J., Burnaby v. Equitable Reversionary Interest Society, 28 C. D. p. 419.

### --- Page 0139 ---

Partition.

INSTRUMENTS

of the recompense) but voidable (y). This common law assurance has fallen out of use, and exchanges are now effected under various Acts of Parliament, or by mutual conveyances.

An equal partition is binding on an infant, because he is compellable by law to make partition, and if the partition be unequal, it is not void but voidable by his entry; and if he take the whole profits of the unequal part after age the partition is made good for ever (2). The different methods of partition are enumerated by Littleton (a); but partitions are now effected by conveyance, or, if any of the parties are infants, or otherwise not sui juris, by recourse to the Chancery Division of the High Court, under the Partition Acts, 1868 and 1874 (0).

The word grant was properly applied to incorporeal hereditaments, of which no livery could be given, as advowsons, services, rents, commons, reversions and such like (c). Besides the authorities before referred to (d), which would make all deeds by an infant void, there is a case (e) which lays down that a grant of an advowson by an infant is void and cannot be confirmed; and this statement is repeated by Brooke (f). On the other hand, we have the rule given by Perkins (g), that an infant’s deed, where the delivery of it answers to the livery of seisin, and operates as the conveyance of an interest, is voidable, approved by Lord Mansfield (h), the authority of Littleton (7), and a decision

(y) Co. Litt. 51 b, where the requisites of an exchange are mentioned.
(z) Co. Litt. 171 a & b.

(c) Co. Litt. 172 a.

(d) Ante, p. 6.

(e) 28 Hen. VIII. 2.

(f) Covert. & Inf. pl. i.; 9 Vin. Ab. 385.

(g) § 12.

(h) Zouch v. Fareons, 3 Burr. 1794.

(2) § 259.

AND ACTS WHICH ARE VOIDABLE. 23

in Hudson v. Jones (1), that the grant of a rentcharge by an infant was voidable. In Holt v. Sundbuch (1) tenant for life and infant remainder-man granted a rentcharge, and the Court inclined to think it good as having been confirmed by the infant, but the case went off on a question of pleading. Stevens v. Bishop of Lincoln (m) is not very intelligible: there was a grant by feme tenant for life and the infant reversioner of the next avoidance of an advowson, and a confirmation by the infant at full age: the feme died, and it was held good during the life of the feme.

By the Act to amend the law of real property (n) corporeal hereditaments lie in grant as well as in livery, and there can be little doubt that an infant’s grant, whether of corporeal or incorporeal hereditaments, and his assignment, would now be regarded as voidable, unless they were necessarily to his prejudice (o).

A grant of an annuity by an infant was formerly void by statute (p), and a judgment entered up against an infant to secure an annuity granted by him would be set aside (q). If an adult joined with an infant in a joint and several grant of an annuity, the grant though void as to the infant was good against the adult (r); though if the grant had been joint only, it might have been altogether void (s). As 53 Geo. III. c. 141 has been repealed (t), an infant’s grant of an annuity now stands on the same footing as his other grants.

An infant may accept a grant, but it is at his election to avoid or confirm it on attaining twenty-one (u). As

### --- Page 0141 ---

Joint tenancy, when severed, by marriage.

INSTRUMENTS

to how far an infant taking property is bound by the conditions attaching to it, whether conditions in fact or in law, see post, p. 78.

It has been said, on the authority of an old case, that an agreement to alien by an infant is not a severance of a joint tenancy in equity; but this case has recently been subjected to criticism by Pearson, J., who held that a proviso in a marriage settlement executed in 1871, for the settlement of the present and after-acquired property of the wife, who was then an infant, and who died soon after attaining twenty-one, without having attempted to repudiate or avoid the settlement, severed a reversionary joint tenancy in consols to which she was then entitled. It would seem, however, that such a proviso or covenant by an infant in a deed executed after the commencement of the Infants Relief Act, 1874, could not be effectually ratified or enforced. Actual alienation is a severance at law, until the alienation is avoided. Before the Married Women’s Property Act, 1882, the marriage of a female effected a severance of her joint tenancy in chattels personal in possession, but not in chattels.

May v. Hook, Co. Litt. 246 a, 0.15 Bac. Ab. Inf, 3.

Burnaby v. Equitable Reversionary Liferent Society, 28 Ch. D. 6. It has been pointed out by a learned judge that if § 2 of the Act applies to a covenant for the settlement of property on an infant's marriage, nothing short of a new disposition of the property by the infant, after majority, would set it up; and such new disposition, unless some fresh valuable consideration were given for it, would be a purely voluntary disposition; see 33 Sol. J. 101; 86 Law Times, 216.

Whittingham’s case, 8 Co. Rep. 43.

45 and 46 Vict. c. 75. § 2 provides that every woman who marries after the commencement of the Act (1st Jan., 1883) shall be entitled to have and to hold as her separate property and to dispose of in manner aforesaid (i.e., by will or otherwise, without the intervention of a trustee; see § 2 (1)) all real and personal property which shall belong to her at the time of marriage, or shall be acquired by or devolve upon her after marriage.

Co. Litt. 185 b; Bracebridge v. Cooke, Plowd. 217.

AND ACTS WHICH ARE VOIDABLE. 25

real, or choses in action which the husband has not reduced into possession during the coverture; but in the case of females married since the commencement of that Act, their joint tenancy will not be thereby severed. If two infant joint tenants join in a conveyance, and one die, the survivor may, by avoiding the conveyance, become entitled to the whole; but if one be adult and die, the infant can only recover a moiety. The Court cannot sever an infant’s joint tenancy.

A lease by an infant, at any rate if rent be reserved, is voidable only. The lease is voidable only, though the best rent is not reserved, and the infant may have an action for rent. Though the infant may set aside the lease, the other party cannot. It is sometimes stated that a lease by an infant without rent is void, the authorities referred to being Blunden v. Baugh and Humphreston’s case. In neither case was this decided; in the former there was a dictum only, while in the latter, where the lease was to try title, the Court differed, and no decision was come to. A lease without reservation of rent is not ipso facto void, for an infant may make a lease to try title; and it may be considered as settled, in accordance with the rule above laid down, that a lease by an infant is void-

### --- Page 0143 ---

Tarol lease.

Determination of tenancy.

Leases to infants,

INSTRUMENTS

able only. If no rent is reserved, unless some benefit thereby accrues to the infant, e.g., trying title, the lease would be held void against the infant as being necessarily to his prejudice; while, on the other hand, though rent is reserved, the lease would be void, if it would work a forfeiture, or cause damage to the infant, or be a breach of trust.

A parol lease is voidable only, for the infant may recover for use and occupation, and may confirm the lease after twenty-one.

An infant cannot determine a tenancy without giving due notice to quit, if it is or has become in any way binding on him, e.g., if he becomes entitled to a reversion of an estate leased from year to year; or if he has brought an ejectment, and the action has been compromised on the terms of the defendant paying arrears of rent and attorning to the infant; or if he has in any way recognized the lease, as by receiving rent; but if the lease be void against the infant, e.g., a lease made by an agent, the infant may bring ejectment without giving notice.

Before the Act of 1874, a lease to an infant was voidable by him, and if he continued to occupy after attaining twenty-one, he was held liable for all arrears of rent; but the covenant to pay rent, even though the lease were ratified after majority, could not now be sued on, nor can an action for use and occupation be

### --- Page 0144 ---

AND ACTS WHICH ARE VOIDABLE. 27

maintained against an infant, except in so far as the Use and occupation can be considered a necessary (y) occupation.

A dictum attributed to Lord Mansfield (2), that if an infant takes an estate, and is to pay rent for it, he shall not hold the estate and defend himself against payment of the rent, by pretence of infancy, has lately been denied (a); and in an action against an infant who had obtained a lease of a furnished house on an implied representation that he was of full age, it was held that the lessor could not at the same time claim to have the lease declared void, and make the infant liable for use and occupation; possession was ordered to be given up, and the infant was ordered to pay the costs of the action (b).

In Conny's case (c) it was held, in an action of Landlord's replevin against a superior lord who had distrained for quit rent, that the attornment of an infant in respect of land taken by descent was good, and the distress was therefore legal. And it is conceived that an infant lessee may still be liable so far as payment of rent is an obligation arising from tenure, though not liable by contract, and therefore that the landlord may pursue any remedy, eg., that of distress, which can be enforced without an action.

Lloyd v. Gregory (d) is often quoted as an authority that a surrender by an infant is void; it appears, however, from another report of that case (e), that, the grant of the new lease being void, the ground of the surrender failed, and it was consequently void as made without consideration. In ordinary

### --- Page 0145 ---

Copyholds,

Covenants in marriage settlements executed before Aug. 7, 1874,

(1) Male infant could not bind real estate.

INSTRUMENTS

In cases a surrender by an infant would be voidable only (f).

An infant is not bound by a surrender of copyholds (g), even though the surrenderee has been admitted (h), unless under special custom (i).

The Act of 1874 (4) has materially altered the law applicable to covenants by infants in marriage settlements; but it is still necessary to consider the cases of settlements executed before that Act, without the statutory authority of the Court (i). It will be convenient to classify the cases under four heads, according as the infant was male or female, and the property real or personal.

(1.) The power of a male infant to bind his realty.

In some of the older cases (m) it seems to have been thought that an infant could bind his estate by a covenant before marriage, if made with the consent of his guardian or parent, or with the sanction of the Court of Chancery; but these were only dicta, and there is no case, it is believed, in the books containing a decision to that effect. Hollingshead v. Hollingshead (2) is an apparent exception, but the settlement in that case had probably been confirmed by the infant when adult (o).

The matter was placed on the true footing in an elaborate judgment of Lord Justice Turner (p); viz,

(f) See Zouch v. Parsons, 3 Burr. 1806; Grange v. Tiving, O. Bridg. 117.

(g) Hughes v. Carpenter, Toth. 278.

(h) Knight v. Footman, 1 Vex. 95, Cro. Eliz. 90.

(i) See Nayler v. Strode, 2 Ch. Rep. 392.

(k) See ante, pp. 19, 24; Vaizey on Settlements, 37.

(l) The Infants Settlement Act, 1855, (18 & 19 Vict. c. 43); see post, p. 338.

(m) Drury v. Drury, 2 Ed. 39, and on appeal, 2 Ed. 60; Larvey v. Ashley, 8 Atk. 607; Seamer v. Bingham, 3 Atk. 54, 56; Cannel v. Buckle, 2 P. W. 243.

(n) Cited 2 P. W. 229.

(o) See 4 B. C. C. 466, and Cotton v. Hoskins, 21 Mar. 1738; 16 Vin. Ab. 486, pl. 3, where the M.R. said it was an idle case, and not law.

(p) Field v. Moore, 7 De G. M. & G. 691, 706.

<br>

### --- Page 0146 ---

AND ACTS WHICH ARE VOIDABLE. 29

that apart from the Statute, the intervention of the Court does not give to the act of an infant any further operation upon property or other legal results than it would have independently of such intervention. Though, however, it may now be considered as settled that (apart from the Statute) a male infant is not bound by his covenant to settle real estate (q), yet it would seem that (under Lord Tenterden’s Act (r)) such a covenant might have been effectually ratified in writing, after the infant had attained twenty-one (s).

If a male infant marry an adult, her ante-nuptial covenant to settle realty is binding on her, and also on her husband, if, after attaining twenty-one, he has accepted benefits under the settlement (¢).

(2.) The power of a male infant to bind his personal estate.

A covenant by a male infant to settle his personal property was voidable by him (w); nor was an agreement by him and his intended (adult) wife for the settlement of her personal estate binding, and it therefore became his on the marriage (2).

(3.) The power of a female infant to bind her real estate.

In spite of some old dicta to the contrary (y), a long list of cases has decided that a female infant cannot bind her real estate (z), and the consent of the guardian

(q) Nightingale v. Nightingale, 3 P. W. 205; Honeywood v. Honeywood, 20 Beav. 451; Trowell v. Shenton, 8 C. D. 318. See also the cases with respect to a female infant, who stands in the same position as a male with respect to her power over real estate.

(r) See ante, p. 19 (x).

(s) Trowell v. Shenton.

(¢) Slocombe v. Glubb, 2 B. C. C. 545; see Kingsman v. Kingsman, 6 Q.B.D. p. 125.

(w) Nelson v. Stocker, 4 De G. & J. 458. See also Beaumont v. Carter, 32 Beav. 586.

(y) Williams v. Williams, 1 B. C. C. 152.

(z) Kingsman v. Kingsman.

(2) Durnford v. Lance, 1 B. C. C. 106; Amfluer v. Harewood, 18 Ves. 259; Lecky v. Knox, 1 Ball & B. 210; Clough v. Clough, 5 Ves. 710;

### --- Page 0147 ---

30.

INSTRUMENTS

or the Court cannot give an infant any power which he does not possess (a).

The late Master of the Rolls, Sir George Jessel, recently stated the principles applicable to a covenant or agreement for a settlement by a female infant (b). He laid it down that an agreement by husband and wife, in an ante-nuptial settlement, for the settlement by the husband and wife of the wife’s after-acquired property, is a covenant by the wife as well as by the husband, whether the wife is a minor or of full age.

If the wife is a minor, and the covenant is for her benefit, it is voidable only and not void, and is binding upon all property coming to her during the coverture for her separate use, without a restraint on anticipation, until she avoids or disaffirms the covenant as to such property; for she may, after attaining twenty-one, and during the coverture, elect whether the covenant shall be binding on her separate estate or not, such right of election being a necessary consequence of a married woman’s power to dispose of, without her husband’s consent, property settled to her separate use: but, inasmuch as a contract by a married woman, while under coverture, affecting her separate property, binds only her then existing separate property, and not separate property which she may thereafter acquire (c), the wife, in electing to confirm the covenant, thereby binds only that separate property to which she

Salisbury v. Bagot, 2 Sw. 608; Stamper v. Barker, 5 Mad. 157; Pim v. Insall, 7 Hare, 193, 1 Mac. & G. p. 454; Simson v. Jones, 2 Russ. & M. 365; Field v. Moore, 7 De G. M. & G. 691; Rawlins v. Birkett, 25 L. J. Ch. 837. But the covenant of the wife is so far operative that it is a fraud in the husband to concur with her in any act to defeat it. Durnford v. Lane, 1 B.C.C. 106; Randall v. Willis, 5 Ves. 262; Pim v. Insall; Exp. Blake, 16 Beav. 463.

Ch. 36 see Sharpe v. Foy, 4; Field v. Moore, 7 De G. M. & G. 710; Blood v. Bramfil, there

(1) Smith v. Lucas, 18 C.D. 531; see also Witdey; Cooper v. Cooper, 18 App. Cas. 1888; Order v. Pisa, 222.

(c) Pike v. Fitzgibbon, 17 C.D. 454.

AND ACTS WHICH ARE VOIDABLE. B1

is entitled at the date of the confirmation, and not that to which she may subsequently become entitled during the coverture. But the doctrine of election or compensation does not apply in the case of a married woman entitled for her separate use with a restraint on anticipation (1).

Under 27 Hen. VIII, c. 10, a female infant’s right to dower may be barred by means of a jointure. The jointure should in strictness be a legal one, but a provision substantially equivalent may be a good bar in equity; it must, however, be a certain and competent provision (e): though the consent of the parent or guardian has been thought to be necessary, the better opinion seems to be that it is only so as evidencing the fairness of the transaction, which might be proved aliunde (f). The law on this subject has become practically obsolete since the Dower Act (g).

(4.) The power of a female infant to bind her personal estate.

In the case of females marrying adults before the commencement of the Married Women’s Property Act, 1882 (h), a valid settlement could be made of their personal estate in possession, or of their choses in action reduced into possession during the coverture; for, as it became the husband’s on marriage, it was in effect his settlement (i), and the fund was bound, even if after marriage the wife did not wish it settled (k); and this

(f) 1 Rop. Husb. and Wife, by Jac. 486; Williams v. Chitty, 3 Ves. 549. As to free bench, see Walker v. Walker, 1 Ves. sen. 54; Willis v. Willis, 34 Beav. 840.

32

Appointment to infant daughter.

Husband can release property which may accrue to wife.

Limitations to voluntaries.

INSTRUMENTS

The case is not affected by the Infants Relief Act, 1874, if the husband is adult at the date of the settlement.

On the same principle, where parents had a life interest in a sum of money, with power to appoint to their children, and on the marriage of an infant daughter appointed the reversion in the fund to trustees, on trusts for the daughter, her intended husband, and the children of the marriage; the daughter and her husband having survived the parents, Lord Romilly held that the appointment to the husband and children was good, though not objects of the power and notwithstanding the infancy of the daughter. He said, “The question of infancy I considered doubtful; but the ground on which Lord St. Leonards puts the case, viz, that the husband can dispose of the property of the wife in expectancy against every one except the wife him surviving, applies just as much to the case of an infant as it does to the case of an adult woman. The ground on which one of the cases seems to have been favourably entertained by the Court, and which applies especially to the case of an infant is this;—that the settlement was one, which, if the infant had been a ward of Court, the Court would have approved of, and which would have been binding on the infant. Accordingly in that view of the case, I think this must be treated on the same principle as Routledge v. Dorril (2 Ves. jun. 357), and that class of cases, in which the fund is considered as first appointed to the daughter, and then settled by the husband for the benefit of the children of the marriage (m).” Similarly the husband may covenant to release property which will accrue to the wife during marriage, and if the property accrue during the coverture, she will be bound by his release (n).

It has been held that a trust of the infant wife’s personal estate for her next of kin is inoperative, and

does not bind her, and that on her husband’s death she can dispose of the property (v). The settlement, being in fact the husband’s, cannot bind a chose in action not reduced into possession during the coverture if the wife survives (p); or if the marriage be dissolved (q); or if the fund falls into possession after a judicial separation or a protection order (7). The same principle applies where the settlement is post-nuptial (s).

If the fund be transferred from trustees, in whose hands it is, to the trustees of the settlement, that is a sufficient reduction into possession to bind the wife (t); but not if the fund merely change hands, without coming into the husband’s possession (w).

But as it is the husband’s covenant which is operative, if the property belong to the wife for her separate use, his covenant does not affect it, and a binding settlement cannot be made (v); and accordingly, it is conceived that, in the case of all marriages on and after January 1st, 1883 (w), the personal estate of a female

### --- Page 0151 ---

34 INSTRUMENTS

The infant can only be effectually settled under the Act of 1855 (x). The old law, however, prevails in the case of settlements by adult husband and infant wife of property which only becomes her separate property by virtue of s. 5 of the Married Women's Property Act, 1882 (y); for by s. 19 it is provided that nothing in the Act contained shall interfere with or affect any settlement or agreement for a settlement made or to be made, whether before or after marriage, respecting the property of any married woman; and therefore, in construing covenants for the settlement of the wife's after-acquired property, the Act is to be disregarded (z), and, as has very recently been held, the persons interested under the settlement are not deprived by s. 5 of the Act of any benefit to which they would have been entitled under the settlement, if that section had not been enacted (a).

It will be seen that the terms of s. 19 of the Act are wide enough to affect the provisions of s. 2 (b); and, when the question arises, it may perhaps be held that even in the case of marriages on and after January 1st, 1883, the adult husband’s covenant to settle the infant wife’s property is effectual, so far as regards property

(x) See post, p. 338.

(y) 45 & 46 Vict. c. 75. This section provides that every woman married before the commencement of the Act shall be entitled to have and to hold and to dispose of in manner aforesaid (i.e., by will or otherwise, without the intervention of a trustee; see sec. 1 (1)) as her separate property, all real and personal property, her title to which, whether vested or contingent, and whether in possession, reversion, or remainder, shall accrue after the commencement of the Act. It is now settled that if such a married woman has before the commencement of the Act acquired a title, whether vested or contingent, and whether in reversion or remainder, to any property, such property is not made her separate estate by this section, though it falls into possession after the commencement of the Act: Reid v. Reid, 31 C. D. 402.

(z) Re Stonor’s Trusts, 24 C. D. 195; Christian v. Whitaker, 34 C. D. 227.

(a) Hancock v. Hancock, 38 ib. 78; see also Re Queade’s Trusts, 36 W. R. 816, disapproved in Hancock v. Hancock.

(b) See ante, p. 24 (5).

AND ACTS WHICH ARE VOIDABLE. 35

which is not expressly given to her for her separate use, because, but for the Act, it would have become his upon the marriage. But it is submitted that this was not the intention of the draftsman, whose marginal note to section 19 (which, however, is not to be taken as part of the Act (c)) is “saving of existing settlements, and the power to make future settlements.”

If both parties are infants, no settlement can be made except under the Statute of 1855 (c).  

In cases not within the Infants Relief Act, 1874, where a female infant is not bound by the settlement, she may, after attaining twenty-one, by acquiescence (c), or by her subsequent conduct (f), confirm it (g). She may confirm it as to a particular fund without confirming it as a whole (i), though in the absence of evidence to the contrary, confirmation of part would be looked on as confirmation of the whole (i). Payment or transfer of a fund to the trustees of the settlement by the direction of the husband and wife (j), or of the wife while a widow (k), is confirmation, but two years’ inaction after the husband’s death is not (k); and it has recently been laid down by the House of Lords that no acts of acquiescence and confirmation can make a post-nuptial settlement by an infant wife of her reversionary interest in personalty binding on her, unless they amount to an actual disposition by her of the property.

Election.

INSTRUMENTS

converted to the trustees of the settlement (m). In Merryweather v. Jones (n) an appointment of new trustees, in which the wife being then over twenty-one joined, was considered to be a confirmation. The property belonged to her for her separate use, and the settlement was a post-nuptial one, executed by her while still an infant.

If a settlement be made conferring benefits on an infant, and purport to include property which cannot be effectually bound, the infant is, in general, put to his or her election whether to claim under the settlement or against it, and if the infant elect not to be bound by it, he or she cannot take any interest under the settlement without compensating those who are disappointed by the refusal to concur. There is, however, one exception to this rule, where a fund is settled to the separate use of a female infant, without power of anticipation, and she has covenanted to settle after-acquired property: in this case she cannot be compelled to elect between after-acquired property and her interest in the settled fund, but may retain both (o). The question of election is a somewhat different one from that of confirmation, for though an election to abide by a settlement is of course a confirmation of it, a confirmation need not raise any case of election, so that part of a settlement only may be confirmed (p), but an election to abide by part is an election to abide by the whole (q). Where there was a post-nuptial settlement with the ordinary

trusts, and a covenant by the husband and wife to settle future property, and after the husband’s death some property fell into possession which the wife refused to settle, she was held bound to recoup to the issue the moneys received by her under the settlement (r). The incapacity to contract arising from infancy stands for this purpose on the same footing as that of coverture (s). If a female infant elects to settle real estate, her heir-at-law taking a benefit under the settlement is put to his election (t). Election will not now be held

Election not to depend on construction.

to depend on questions of construction as to whether the provisions made on the husband’s side are intended to be conditional on the wife's fund being brought into settlement in any event. “It appears to me, therefore, that the only safe rule to guide the Court in dealing with marriage settlements is to consider everything that is brought or expressed to be brought into settlement by anybody from any source as one aggregate trust fund. So considering it, it seems to me very easy and very right to read all the trusts thus:—Out of the aggregate property settled, A is to have so much and no more, B is to have so much and no more, the issue are to have such interests and no more or other. Then if by paramount title A or B, or any of the issue, takes out of the aggregate something other than the share or interest expressed to be given, he or she must take that in full satisfaction of the expressed share or interest. This would seem to me to give a very simple rule, to which every one of the cases can be referred except Campbell v. Ingilby (u), which I more than doubt the

propriety of” (v). In the case referred to (w), by a postnuptial settlement certain stock belonging to the husband, and bank shares belonging to the wife’s father, were settled on the husband and wife, and the husband and wife purported to assign a reversionary interest in certain funds to the trustees of the settlement upon similar trusts. The marriage was dissolved by a decree of the Divorce Court, and afterwards the wife’s reversion fell into possession, and it was held that her purported assignment was sufficient to raise a case of election against her between the interests provided for her by the settlement and her right to receive the fund free from the settlement (2).

AND ACTS WHICH ARE VOIDABLE.

limitation in default of issue to the next of kin of the wife. The wife died soon after the marriage without issue, and without having executed a settlement, leaving her sister her heir and one of her next of kin. The sister refused to bring the real estate into the settlement; it was held that as the husband would have been absolutely entitled to his wife’s personal estate but for the settlement, if the real estate were not brought into settlement he had a right to claim compensation out of the personal property against all the next of kin. The case of Campbell v. Ingilby (a) was a very similar one; the only difference being that the wife exercised the power of appointment, and died at the age of forty-nine, while in Suvill v. Savill she died a month after attaining her majority. The husband filed his bill to have the trusts of the settlement executed, or if the real estate could not be brought into settlement, that compensation might be made him out of the wife’s personalty. Lord Romilly, M.R., dismissed the bill on the ground that, the primary consideration for the whole contract being marriage, because a collateral consideration failed, the contract could not be varied subsequently; and he expressly distinguished the case from Suvill v. Savill on the ground that appointees of the wife stood in a different position from the next of kin taking in default of appointment, being as against the husband purchasers under the settlement. The case was affirmed on a totally different ground. They held that the husband must be taken to have known that the settlement by the wife did not bind the real estate, and ought to have made her levy a fine; so that, as for twenty-eight years he had not done so, he had, in fact, acquiesced in the omission of the real estate from the settlement; but, Turner, LJ., expressly refused to decide any question of election, and said he was not satisfied whether the husband could originate such a

(a) 21 Beav. 567; 1 De G. & J. 293.

40

Extent of Rule as to election,

No election during disability.

INSTRUMENTS

claim, though it might be good as a defence. Lord Romilly, in Brown v. Brown (b), admitted that some of his observations in Campbell v. Ingilby went too far, but he referred the decision to the principle, that as the heir claimed no benefit under the settlement, the question of election did not arise. It is difficult to see how the distinction between the appointees and the next of kin can be maintained, both being collaterals claiming through the wife; and if so, Lord Romilly’s judgment in Campbell v. Ingilby can hardly be reconciled with Savill v. Savill. Campbell v. Ingilby was disapproved of by James, L. J. (c), and the principles on which Lord Romilly’s judgment was based would not probably now be followed. As regards the observation that, as the heir of the wife did not claim any benefit under the settlement, no case of election arose, it seems a sufficient answer, that if the husband would have a right to put the wife to her election, and in the event of her refusing to settle the real estate, to receive compensation out of her personalty, it is hard to see how that right can be destroyed on her death, merely because her real estate goes to one person and her personal estate to another, both of whom claim through her (d).

It will be observed that the rule laid down in Codrington v. Lindsay (e) is a wider one than the principle on which Savill v. Savill was decided; in that case, but for the settlement, the husband would have been absolutely entitled by marriage to the wife’s personalty, while in the former case, as the reversionary interest did not fall into possession during the coverture, the husband had no claim outside the settlement.

The election cannot be made while disability continues (f), but with respect to property belonging to her

AND ACTS WHICH ARE VOIDABLE.

For her separate use, a married woman is on the same footing as a feme sole (g), and she may act so that it would be a fraud in her afterwards to refuse to execute the settlement (4). If a case of election in respect of personal estate arise against the wife, and she die before exercising any election, each of her next of kin has a separate right to elect, and is not bound by the election of her administrator (i).

The capacity of infants to execute powers was much discussed in two recent cases (j), where all the authorities were reviewed. The result appears to be that an infant may, by instrument inter vivos, but not by will (k), execute a power simply collateral, over real or personal estate, if (to quote the language of Wood, V.-C.) he is “a mere conduit pipe, as it has been termed, of the will of the donor of the power; so that when the estate is created, the infant (as was said in Bridgman) is merely the instrument by whose hands the testator or donor acts. The donor, it is said, may use any hand however weak to carry out his intentions” (f). There is no other kind of power which can be exercised over real estate by an infant (m): so that an infant with a discretionary power of sale, or trustee for sale, though he have no beneficial interest, cannot sell (nm). In Grange v. Tiving (o) the settlor covenanted to stand seised upon trusts for himself and issue, and the reserved power to himself and his heirs to revoke the settlement,

(k) See the Wills Act, (1 Vict. c. 26) s. 7.

INSTRUMENTS

and appoint new uses. After his death his infant daughter purported to execute the power, and it was held that she might do so as far as the revoking the old uses was concerned; but this decision was not approved of by Lord St. Leonards.

Power coupled with an interest. An infant can execute a power over personal estate, even though coupled with an interest which is affected by its exercise (a power in gross) where an intention appears that it should be exercisable during minority; and even without such an intention, a power over personal estate coupled with an interest which cannot be affected by its exercise (a power appendant or appurtenant) seems to be exercisable by an infant.

As to powers in a settlement made under the Infants Settlement Act, see post, chap. xix. An infant may of course execute a power, if he is authorised to do so by Act of Parliament.

As an infant cannot bind himself by deed, so he is not bound by a recital in a deed, or estopped by a deed; but an infant may not, after majority, set up any title to property accidentally omitted from her marriage settlement, executed under the provisions of the Infants Settlement Act, 1855, adversely to the trustees thereof, on the ground that she ought to make good the representations upon the faith of which the marriage and the settlement had been sanctioned by the Court.

2. As to instruments not under seal and parol agreements.

Even under the old law an infant was not liable.

<br>

### --- Page 0160 ---

AND ACTS WHICH ARE VOIDABLE. 

his bill of exchange or promissory note, either as drawer or acceptor; but his acceptance was formerly voidable only, and he might ratify it after attaining twenty-one. The acceptor must, however, prove that he was an infant at the date of acceptance; and though the bill bears a date subsequent to his attaining twenty-one, it is sufficient if he shows that he was an infant when he accepted the bill, the date being left blank, and that a wrong date was inserted. An acceptance is binding if given when the acceptor was an adult, though the bill was drawn while he was an infant. Under the Infants Relief Act, 1874, a renewal after majority of a promissory note given whilst an infant, otherwise than by way of payment for necessaries, is a mere ratification of a void contract, and the note could only be sued on by a bona fide holder for value. An infant cannot be sued on a note given for a loan to be expended, and afterwards expended, on necessaries. 

It is a general principle applicable to all negotiable securities, that a person shall not dispute the power of another to indorse such an instrument, when he asserts by the instrument which he issues to the world that the other has such power. Accordingly, it is no answer to an action by an indorsee against the drawer or acceptor, that the payee of the bill who

Purchase of goods, when liability to be determined.

Breach of contract cannot be converted into tort.

INSTRUMENTS

indorsed it over was an infant (hk); though it might be different if the infant had avoided his endorsement (i).

An infant’s liability or non-liability for goods sold to him must be determined at the time the property in the goods passes to him; and, as goods delivered to a carrier selected by the purchaser become his property from the moment of such delivery (k), where goods are bought by an infant, and delivered to his carrier, but not delivered by the carrier to him till after he has attained twenty-one, he is not liable on the contract ().

Though an infant is liable for a tort (m), yet if the transaction be one of contract, he cannot be made liable as for a tort (vn); but an infant over fourteen years of age may be convicted of larceny as a bailee under 24 & 25 Vict., c. 96, s. 3, for fraudulently converting to his own use goods delivered to him under a hiring agreement (o).

Though a contract made by an infant is not binding on him, it is, at all events in cases not within the recent Act (p), binding on the other party, at the infant’s option, for “those who enter into contracts with infants shall be bound, if it be prejudicial to the infant to rescind the contract” (q); so that where there are mutual covenants between an infant and an adult, the infant may sue for the breach, though he is not bound by his own covenant (7). An infant may issue a debtor’s summons in his own name (s); sue on
