Implied contract with infant taken away by express contract with third person.

No liability where infant is already supplied.

CONTRACTS

arrest, or to save him from ejectment for non-payment of rent. Necessaries for an infant’s wife are necessaries for him; and on the same principle an infant’s widow may bind herself to pay for her husband’s funeral. Costs of a marriage settlement, and of proceedings to protect the infant’s estate, are necessaries, and, for a person in good position, presents of jewellery to his intended bride.

The cost of boarding and lodging an infant may be recovered from him on an implied contract, but an express agreement by a third person to pay takes away such implied contract.

A question has not infrequently arisen, whether, if an infant be already supplied with necessary articles, a tradesman who (whether knowing of the existing supply or not) supplies other goods of a similar description, can recover the price from the infant. After some difference of judicial opinion, this question has lately been set at rest by two decisions, one of the Queen’s Bench Division, and one of three of the Lords Justices sitting as a Divisional Court, following the earlier cases which were disapproved by the Court of Exchequer in Ryder v. Wombwell. In such a case the tradesman cannot recover. The law in America is in accordance with this conclusion.

Though an infant may contract to pay for necessaries,

### --- Page 0210 ---

FOR NECESSARIES. 93

he cannot do so by bond with a penalty, or by bond, &c., account stated, or by bill of exchange, or promissory note, or, it seems, by cognovit in an action against him for necessaries; nor can he give a valid security over his property to secure the repayment of money advanced for necessaries. It has been said that he may bind himself by a single bond, or bill of exchange, or promissory note, payable only to the person who supplied the necessaries. Money lent to an infant to pay for necessaries could not formerly be recovered at law, though the infant spent it on necessaries afterwards; secus, if it was laid out by the lender in necessaries for him; and payment for necessaries for an infant was held good payment to him. Where groceries were supplied to an infant trader, the vendor recovered for such as had been consumed by the infant and his family, as necessaries. But money lent to an infant to pay for necessaries could always be recovered in equity, and the equitable doctrine now prevails. An acknowledgment by an infant of a debt for necessaries is sufficient to take it out of the Statute of Limitations.

A contract of service is looked upon as in general.

Contracts of

CONTRACTS

beneficial to the infant(x), and accordingly an infant may bind himself as an apprentice, or by a contract of service(3); unless the contract is one manifestly to his disadvantage, e.g., if the master may stop the work whenever he chooses and retain the wages during the stoppage(t); or is not to be liable to pay wages while the business is interrupted by a “turn-out”(uv). But the question really is whether the provisions of the contract are such as were at the time common to labour contracts, or in the then condition of trade such as the master was reasonably justified in imposing for his own protection, and whether the wages were a fair remuneration for the infant’s services(v).

If the master be an infant, such a contract is voidable, not void(wv). The contract may be entered into with the father(x), or mother of the infant(y).

A father has no common law right to bind his son an apprentice(z); the infant must himself execute the deed(a), but he may do so by an agent(b).

The contract is put an end to by the death of the master(c). His executor cannot maintain an action against the apprentice, unless he has agreed to serve “the executor(d)”; as he may do(e). The contract may be terminated on the master’s bankruptcy by notice to

OF SERVICE. 95

the trustee from either party. There is an implied stipulation that the contract is to be performed at the place where the business is carried on and the parties reside at the date of the contract. It may be put an end to by a material alteration in the master’s position; if, for instance, the master have several trades and give up one of them; if the apprentice be bound to two partners and one of them die, or retire from business; or if he is bound to four partners, and, after dissolution, two set up business in another place, and the other two remain, but neither firm carries on the original business in its entirety.

The contract is terminated by the death of the apprentice; or by his becoming unable to serve by the act of God, as by permanent illness. He cannot avoid the contract himself, while an infant, even with the consent of the master, unless it is clearly for his benefit that the contract should be dissolved. The master cannot, unless there is a stipulation to that effect, terminate it on account of the apprentice’s misconduct, except in cases of habitual misconduct, which might damage the master; as if the master were a watchmaker, and there was

often gold lying about, and the apprentice were a thief (7°); or the master being a doctor, if the apprentice by his carelessness endanger the master’s practice (8). In that case, however, the defendant was a “pupil and assistant,” and different in some degree from an apprentice. The infant’s absenting himself from the master’s service is not an avoidance of the contract (t).

Though an apprenticeship deed is binding on an infant, the master cannot sue on the covenant to serve (uu); except under the custom of London, which enables an infant above fourteen to bind himself by such a covenant, and this may be sued on in any Court (v). The master may moderately chastise the infant for misconduct (w); but the ordinary course is to apply to the magistrates (x), under the Statutes relating to Master and Servant (y). A conviction by the magistrates is a sufficient answer to a habeas corpus to have an apprentice discharged from custody; if the conviction is wrong, the remedy is against the magistrates (2).

As an infant apprentice cannot be sued on his covenant, it is usual for the father to be a party to the indenture, and to covenant that the son shall serve. He is liable for the son’s default on this covenant (a).

though the son avoid the deed at twenty-one (b). As the son’s default does not put an end to the indenture, damages will only be given up to the time of bringing the action (c). The fact that the master took no trouble to bring back an apprentice, who absented himself, is no answer to the action, though it may mitigate the damages (d). Any of the grounds which would justify a dissolution of the contract would be a sufficient answer (e).

The master may have an action against any one who seduces away his apprentice (f), or injures him (g).

As the master has a right to the apprentice’s earnings, if the latter leave him and earn money, the master has a right to such earnings (h), and the apprentice cannot sue for them (i), or the master may have an action for the value of the apprentice’s services (k).

The master’s right does not extend to extraordinary gains by the apprentice, as prize-money won by him, but only to his ordinary wages for service and labour (l).

The apprentice may avoid the contract at twenty-one (m). He may apply to the magistrate under the statutes above cited. He may have an action against the master on his covenant to teach (n), but as dismissal for misconduct does not put an end to the contract, he can only recover damages from the pre-

Recovery of premium,

CONTRACTS

tended dismissal up to the time of bringing the action. It is a sufficient answer to such action that the apprentice would not be taught. An infant may sue in the County Court for his wages, not exceeding 50/, in the same manner as if he were of full age, that is, without a next friend.

Where the contract is put an end to, the question arises whether the apprentice can recover any part of the premium. After the bankruptcy of the master, the trustee may, on application by or on behalf of the apprentice, repay such sum as he thinks reasonable, having regard to the amount of the premium, and to the time the apprentice has served; or transfer the indenture of apprenticeship to another person. Under the Employers and Workmen Act, 1875, if a court of summary jurisdiction rescinds the instrument of apprenticeship, it may, if it thinks it just so to do, order the whole or any part of the premium to be repaid. In some old cases, where the indenture had been set aside by the Mayor's Court, or on the death of the master, and in one case where the apprentice was dismissed for misconduct, the Court of Chancery ordered part of the premium to be returned, but these cases are opposed to Argles v. Heaseman, and Hale v. Webb; and it has recently been held that the

Sometimes no deed of apprenticeship is executed, but the infant is taken on trial, and is afterwards to be apprenticed. Under various statutes, known as the Poor Law Acts, parochial authorities (7) have power to apprentice pauper children over eight (f) and under twenty-one (g).

As to the proper persons to sign the indenture of apprenticeship, see 3 & 4 Will. IV. c. 63; and as to provisions for keeping a register of such apprentices, see 42 Geo. III. c. 46; 7 & 8 Vict. c. 101, s. 12; 14 Vict. c. 11, ss. 3, 5.

### --- Page 0217 ---

Contracts for an infant’s

May be declared binding by the Court.

CONTRACTS BINDING ON INFANT.

is an infant. Other Acts contain provisions for punishing apprentices for misconduct and for protecting their rights. For a full discussion on the subject the reader is referred to treatises on the Poor Laws. According to Buller, J., it was laid down by Lord Mansfield, in E. of Buckinghamshire v. Drury, that “if an agreement be for the benefit of the infant at the time, it shall bind him,” and he said that Lord Hardwicke afterwards adopted this rule; but Jessel, M. R., denied that either of these judges had laid down any such general principle; and it is submitted that, if there be any foundation for the statement, the meaning of the supposed rule is not that an infant can bind himself by a beneficial contract as he may by a contract for necessaries, but that if it become necessary to decide the question, the Court may decree such an agreement to be binding. As it is for an infant’s benefit to do voluntarily what he might be compelled by suit to do, it has been laid down that “whatsoever an infant is bound to do by law the same shall bind him, albeit he doth it without suit of law.” Thus he is bound by his attornment during infancy, or by conveyance of an estate in mortgaged property, the mortgage being paid off, and in such a case Lord Eldon said a Court of equity would restrain him from trying to avoid his conveyance. In practice, however, this doctrine is never relied on, but a conveyance or vesting order is obtained under the Trustee Acts; see post, ch. xxxii.

### --- Page 0218 ---

FRAUD, 101

The legal doctrine that an infant cannot make a contract was carried so far, that he was not bound at law, even if he induced the other party to enter into the contract by a fraudulent representation that he was of age (y).

In equity, however, it is held that if an infant be old enough to commit a fraud by inducing others to think that he is of age, he cannot take advantage of it, and if the Court cannot restore the parties to their original footing, the infant will be bound as if he were an adult (z). Thus where a father tenant for life represented himself as entitled in fee, and the tenant in tail in remainder, then eighteen years old, himself engrossed a mortgage deed made by his father, and did not disclose his title, he was held bound by the mortgage (a).

A lease made fraudulently was only set aside on the infant’s repaying the premium (b). An infant’s wife was held liable on two promissory notes made before her marriage; the husband, having induced the creditor to give them up in exchange for his bond, was ordered to restore the notes, and restrained from pleading the Statute of Limitations (c). Payment by trustees to an infant who represented himself to be of age was held good (d); so also a release given under similar circumstances (e), and a bond for an advance made to

N. 8. 258; De Roo v. Foster, 12 ib. 272; Bartlett v. Wells, 1 B. & S. 836; Miller v. Blankney, 38 L. T. 537.

102 FRAUD.

him (e). The equitable doctrine now prevails (f); but it is doubtful whether since the Infants Relief Act, 1874, an infant can be adjudicated a bankrupt for a trading debt, even if he has expressly represented himself to the petitioning creditor as of full age (9).

If an infant asserts that he is of age, and another acts upon it, the infant is bound, whether such assertion is to his knowledge untrue or not (hk); but if the other party knows that he is an infant, and so is not deceived, the infant is not bound (7).

It is not always easy to decide what amounts to fraud. It seems there must be some active conduct on the part of the infant, as in all the cases quoted above; allowing another person to deal with him as if he were an adult, or doing acts which only an adult can properly do, is not sufficient (%). Allowing his name to be painted up over the door (i), or being gazetted as partner (m), is not sufficient to bind him as a partner; though in such a case a Court of equity would probably not give him any active assistance (7).

An infant known to the other party to be so is not bound by acquiescence, as by allowing another to act on the faith that the infant will do or not do certain things (0), but if the acquiescence or untrue

TORTS. 103

The representation continue after twenty-one he will be bound (o).

It does not fall within the scope of this treatise to discuss how far an infant may commit crimes (p). He is liable for torts and injuries of a private nature; and the employer of an infant servant may in some cases be liable for the infant's torts (g). An action grounded on contract cannot be converted into tort, e.g., he is not liable on a warranty of goods sold (r); or for over-riding a horse which he had hired (s); but where he was expressly told not to jump the horse, and did so, and injured him, it was held to be as much a tort as if he had taken the horse out of the plaintiff's stable without leave (t). He is liable for trespass (w), for slander (x), for wrongfully detaining goods (y), or embezzling money (z); and may be restrained (with costs) from selling spurious articles, represented by him to have been manufactured by the plaintiff (a).

### --- Page 0221 ---

Office of public and pecuniary trust,

Ministerial office.

CHAPTER V.

POWER OF AN INFANT TO FILL AND EXERCISE VARIOUS POSITIONS AND DUTIES.

Section 1—Under the General Law.

AN infant cannot hold any office of public and pecuniary trust, such as Clerk to the Court of Requests, where it was part of his duty to receive the money of the suitor (a); he cannot be bailiff to the sheriff (b), or bailiff or receiver (c). He cannot be a priest or deacon (d), vote as a peer (e), or be elected as or vote for a member of Parliament (f); and he was held unable to perform the service of grand sergeantry (g). He cannot be enrolled as a burgess of a corporation (h), or councillor (i), alderman (k), or mayor (l). He may hold any office that is merely minis-

Bradley, 18 C. B. N. S. 65; Hargreaves v. Hopper, 1 C. P. D. 195; 48 Vict. c. 15, 2nd Sched., Form 1, I, 4; 3rd Sched., Form of Precept, I. 3.

POSITIONS AND DUTIES. 105

terial, as clerk of the peace (m), or even it seems an office requiring skill, such as sheriff (x), or gaoler (o), parker or steward (p), registrar to a bishop (q); at any rate, if he is able to perform the duties by deputy; but if he appoints an insufficient deputy, it would be a forfeiture (r). It has been laid down that an infant steward of a manor cannot be a steward of a manor (s). This was much discussed in the case of Edlestone v. Collins (t), and the Court seemed to think that an infant might be a steward either in his own person, or, if too young, by deputy; and, at any rate, an act performed by him in such office, such as taking the surrender of a married woman, was not necessarily void (u).

An infant may be an agent, and the principal is bound by his acts (a).

An infant may be lord of a manor, and make grants of the copyhold land, for the estate of the copyholder is not derived out of the estate or interest of the lord of the manor; he is but an instrument to make the grant (y). On the same principle he was allowed to appoint a seneschal to preside over the Manor Court (z).

The courts should be held in the name of the guardian; so long, at any rate, as the infant lord is under fourteen (a).

An infant may be a shareholder in a railway company, member of a company or society,

### --- Page 0224 ---

INFANT TRUSTEE. 107

to an infant (7); but the Court of Chancery would not pay money out of Court to an infant executor (8).

An infant may be appointed a trustee (t); but he cannot exercise any power which requires the application of prudence and discretion. An infant devisee in trust for sale, even if he have no beneficial interest, cannot sell (w). The Court will appoint a new trustee in his place (x); but the appointment will be without prejudice to an application by him to be restored to the trusteeship on attaining twenty-one (y). The Court has jurisdiction, though the infant is of unsound mind (z).

An infant trustee cannot be made liable for a breach of trust (a), unless it is a continuing breach, in which he acquiesces after attaining twenty-one (b). He is answerable for a breach committed after he attained his majority (c), and, semble, for moneys actually misappropriated during his infancy (d).

Section 2.—Enabling Statutes.

The disabilities of infants have in some cases been removed by Statute.

### --- Page 0226 ---

CHAPTER VI.  
REMEDIES FOR THE PROTECTION OF INFANTS.

Section 1.—As to the Person.

In so far as an infant is entitled to the same remedies to protect his rights as an adult, it is not intended to discuss them, but there are some special points which require notice.

First, As to the person of the infant.

The object of a writ of habeas corpus is the protection of the liberty of the party; it will not therefore be granted to a master to bring up an infant servant without the latter’s authority (a). The infant himself who is detained in custody may have it, or the father (b) or legal guardian (c); and in one case, the sister who was the nearest relative of an orphan girl under fourteen was allowed it (d). It will not be given to a stranger (e). If there be no one entitled to the custody, perhaps the writ might be obtained in the name of the Attorney-General, as representing the Crown, parens patriae, but the better plan is to make the infant a ward of Court (f).

An infant may have an action of trespass for assault and false imprisonment, but there must be some proof of actual restraint exercised upon him (g).

An infant, like an adult, may have an action in many cases for injuries to his person; as for instance, injury injuries.

(a) R. v. Edwards, 7 T. R. 745; R. v. Reynolds, 6 T. R. 497.  
(b) Post, ch. vii. s. 4.  
(c) Post, ch. xxvi. s. 1.  
(d) Re Daley, 2 F. & F. 258.  
(e) Re Fitzgerald, 2 Com. L. R. 1801.  
(f) Post, ch. vii. s. 3.  
(g) Herring v. Boyle, 1 C. M. & R. 377.

### --- Page 0227 ---

Injuries as a servant,  
Common employment.  

Caused by his own negligence,  

REMEDIES FOR THE  

caused by a spring gun placed upon land on which the infant trespassed (k). He may recover damages for the loss and injury to himself, but not for loss to others: thus an infant who had been bitten by a dog and sued for damages, could not recover the amount of the doctor’s bill, which had been paid by his father; secus, if it had been paid with the infant's own money (i).

An infant servant is within the rule that the master is not liable for injuries occurring in the ordinary course of his employment; and also within the rule as to “common employment,” which prevents a servant from suing the master for damages for injuries caused by a fellow-servant (l); but the Employers Liability Act, 1880, which is expressly extended to infants (2), has considerably modified the common law rule in favour of the servant.

An infant cannot, any more than an adult, recover damages, if the injury has been caused by his own negligence. Thus if a tradesman has a cellar, opening by a flap on to the street, he must take reasonable care that the flap is placed securely, or he will be liable for injury caused by it (m); but he will not be liable, if the plaintiff caused the flap to fall (n). So where a child of three years and a half old got on a railway and was injured, no negligence being shown against the Company, they were held not liable (o).

(h) Jay v. Whitfield, cited 4 Bing. 644, 3 B. & Ald. 508.  

(i) Collins v. Lefevre, 1 F. & F. 436.

Frost, 3 F. & F. 622 (where, on the ground that the owner of dangerous machinery is bound to take care that young persons employed about it are duly instructed as to its nature and use, a master was held liable for injuries sustained by an infant in using it), and Fist v. Cent. Pacific R.R. Co., 1 Amer. St. Rep. 22, 28, n.

### --- Page 0228 ---

PROTECTION OF INFANTS. 111

It is said (p) that though the defendant is not liable, if the injury be caused entirely by the infant’s negligence, he is liable if the infant has only been guilty of contributory negligence. The distinction is a fine one. In Lynch v. Nurdin (q) the defendant left a horse and cart standing in the road, and the plaintiff, a child of seven, having climbed into it and been injured, recovered damages. This case was doubted by Pollock, C. B. (r) ; and Parke, B., said it was decided on the ground that the plaintiff had taken as much care as could be expected from a child of tender years; and Alderson, B., suggests that the negligence is really that of the parent, who allows the child to be at large (s).

A child of tender years is so identified with the person it is with, that if the negligence of the latter cause an injury the child cannot recover (t). A child injured by the negligence of a Railway Company does not lose its right to recover damages by the fact that the person in charge of it has not taken a ticket, at any rate if there be no fraud (u).

Damages recovered by an infant may be ordered to be paid into Court for his benefit; see ante, p. 56.

Where the infant dies after verdict, but before judgment, see Kramer v. Waymark (v).

Numerous Statutes known as the Factories Acts have been passed to limit the hours during which in Employment in Factories,

(q) 1 Q. B. 29.

(r) Lygo v. Newbold, 9 Exch. 362; in Lay v. Midland Railway Co., 34 L. T. 30, Pollock, C. B., intimated that what would be considered contributory negligence in an adult might not be so regarded in the case of a young child; see on this point Kerr v. Forgue, 5 Amer. Rep. 146; Lynch v. Smith, 6 ib. 188; Bliss v. Sowh Hadley, 1 Amer. St. Rep. 441; Moebus v. Herrmann, 2 ib. 440.

(s) See also, on this point, JAl v. Forty-second Street Ferry Co., 7 Amer. Rep. 450; Cosgrove v. Ogden, 10 ib. 361.

### --- Page 0229 ---

112

Industrial Schools,

Person entering an estate treated as bailiff.

Sand,

REMEDIES FOR THE

children and young persons may work in factories, and for their protection. They have been amended and consolidated by 41 Vict. c. 16, and 46 & 47 Vict. c. 53, s. 14. Special Statutes apply to their employment in shops (v), mines, whether coal (w), or metal (x), agricultural gangs (y), chimney sweeping (z), and dangerous performances (a). Under the Industrial Schools Acts and the Reformatory Schools Acts, children may be detained in these schools for various periods, as directed by the proper authority.

We may here also refer to 24 & 25 Vict. c. 100, 43 & 44 Vict. c. 45, and 48 & 49 Vict. c. 69, for the punishment of various offences against women and children; and to 35 & 36 Vict. c. 38, for the protection of infants put out to nurse. By 49 & 50 Vict. c. 56, the sale of liquors to children under thirteen years old for consumption on the premises by children under that age is made illegal. By 52 & 53 Vict. c. 44, stringent provisions are made for the purpose of preventing and punishing the ill-treatment and neglect of children, and restrictions are placed on their employment (b).

Section 2.—Remedies for the Protection of Property.

It is a rule in equity that an infant is entitled to treat another who takes possession (c) of his estate as his “bailiff” or agent, and to obtain from him an account of the rents and profits, and a decree for possession.

(v) 49 & 50 Vict. c. 55.  
(w) 35 & 36 Vict. c. 76.  
(x) 35 & 36 Vict. c. 77.  
(y) 30 & 31 Vict. c. 130.  
(z) 3 & 4 Vict. c. 85; 27 & 28 Vict. c. 37.  
(a) 42 & 43 Vict. c. 34.  
() See also 39 & 40 Vict. c. 79, ss. 5-9.

(c) In Wall v. Stanwick, 34 C. D. 763, a mother, entitled to a house during widowhood only, remarried and remained in possession. It was held that her children, who became entitled on her marriage, and continued to live with her, might treat her as their bailiff, and charge her with an occupation rent, but such sums as might properly be allowed for their maintenance and education were to be set off against the rent.

<br>

### --- Page 0230 ---

PROTECTION OF INFANTS. 113
sion. This rule was laid down by Lord Hardwicke (c), and is supported by a long series of cases (d), whether the person who enters be, as in most of the cases, the father, or mother (e), or a stranger (f). It is immaterial whether the infant is entitled at law or only in equity (g), or whether he has ever been in possession or not (h). On this principle a co-tenant in common with an infant, if he occupy the whole, must pay an occupation rent (i); and if a person jointly interested with him renew a lease, it enures to the benefit of the infant, if beneficial (k). He may bring an action of assumpsit for rents received by the stranger (l). As it is a privilege for an infant to treat the occupier as his bailiff in equity instead of bringing an action of trespass at law, he must do equity, and proceed against all the occupiers, and not one only (m).

As the infant is entitled to treat the occupier as a trustee for him (n), he may have an account of mesne profits during all the time the other has been in possession (o); and not merely for six years, as the Statutes of Limitations relating to land do not apply (p); but

114 REMEDIES FOR THE

any proper expenditure will be allowed to the occupier (q), and the person charged as bailiff is not liable for permissive waste (r). The marriage of a female infant, even before 1883 (s), did not change the character of the occupier’s possession as the infant's bailiff (t); nor is that character changed when the infant attains twenty-one (u). The account will not be limited to the time when the infant attained twenty-one, if the action be brought within a reasonable time afterwards; but if there be a delay, the Court lays hold of small circumstances to show that the account has been waived (x); and if more than six years have elapsed since the infant attained twenty-one, his right to an account will be barred (y).

Operation of Statutes of Limitations. It has been doubted whether this rule would enable an infant in all cases, for the purpose of escaping from the effect of the Statutes of Limitations, to treat as “bailiff for him” a stranger who has entered upon his land (z). It was held by Sir J. Leach that the rule did not apply to a stranger who made an adverse entry (a). In a recent case in Ireland (b) the Vice-Chancellor considered that the rule only applied where the person who entered was (1) natural guardian of the infant, or (2) so connected with him by relationship or otherwise as to have imposed on him the duty to protect, or at any rate not to prejudice the infant's rights, or (3) when he took possession with knowledge of the infant’s rights; and he suggested that the judg-

### --- Page 0232 ---

PROTECTION OF INFANTS,

ment in Crowther v. Crowther (c) was intended to apply to a case of simple adverse possession; but in Wall v. Stanwick it was said by Kekewich, J., that it is presumed that the person in possession is the infant’s bailiff wherever circumstances require it, that is, wherever it is proper to make a man accountable for the rents and profits of an infant’s estate, and he cannot be shown to have been in possession in some other character (d).

Where the infant can treat the person in possession as his bailiff, time does not begin to run against him till he attains twenty-one, and his title is not barred till the expiration of twelve years from that date; in cases in which the doctrine cannot be applied, the title may be barred six years after the infant has attained twenty-one (e).

Entry by an heir-at-law relates back to the time when his title accrued, so that he may bring an action for a trespass committed before his entry (f).

Trover for goods belonging to an infant should be brought in his name, not that of his father (g). An infant may bring an action for money had and received, against an attorney who has recovered damages on his behalf (h); he may bring an action for wages (i).

Section 3.—Protection after Twenty-one.

The protection afforded by law to infants, in disabling them from executing binding instruments, is extended by Courts of Equity in many cases after they have

### --- Page 0233 ---

116

Voluntary settlements,  

Undue influence.  

Unconscionable bargains.  

REMEDIES FOR THE

attained twenty-one, until they have all the information which might have been acquired in adult years (k).

A person who has just attained twenty-one may make a voluntary settlement on himself, but those who induce him to execute it “are bound to show either that the deed is in all respects proper, or if the deed contains anything out of the way, that he understood and approved of it” (l). It cannot stand unless it represents the deliberate and unbiased intention of the settlor, formed after full information: such a deed is accordingly by no means easy to support, if the settlor afterwards wishes to get rid of it (m).

These considerations apply with greater force where the deed is not intended to be solely for the infant's benefit, but confers advantages on others. Thus a mining lease was set aside, made eleven months after the infant attained twenty-one, not being on the best terms, and not being shown to have been fully understood and intended by her (n).

We may here refer to the cases of unconscionable bargains with expectant heirs or young men of fortune. In the earlier cases the equity to relief was grounded on the fact that the plaintiff's interest was a reversion or expectancy, or that the bargain was a fraud on the person from whom the fortune was expected (o). In more recent cases, however, to quote the language of Lord Selborne, L. C., “whatever weight there may be in any such collateral considerations, they could hardly prevail, if they did not connect themselves with an equity more strictly and directly personal to the plaintiff.”

(k) Walker v. Symonds, 3 Sw. 69.  
(l) Phillips v. Muddlings, 7 Ch. 244, 247.  
(m) Everitt v. Everitt, 10 Ey. 405; Pridaux v. Lonsdale, 1 De G. J. & S. 433. As to the less strict rule in cases of family arrangements, see post, p. 185.  
(n) Grosvenor v. Sherratt, 28 Beav. 659; and see Say v. Barwick, 1 V. & B. 195. As to gifts to parents, see post, ch. x, § 2; to guardians or others in a fiduciary position, post, ch. xxviii, § 8.  
(o) See post, ch. x, § 4.

<br>

### --- Page 0234 ---

PROTECTION OF INFANTS.

in each particular case. But the real truth is, that the ordinary effect of all the circumstances by which these considerations are introduced, is to deliver over the prodigal helpless into the hands of those interested in taking an advantage of his weakness; and we so arrive in every such case at the substance of the conditions, which throw the burden of justifying the righteousness of the bargain upon the party who claims the benefit of it” (p). Though the youth and inexperience of the victim is not an essential element in the case, it is an important one, as showing that “the parties meet under such circumstances as in the particular transaction to give the stronger party dominion over the weaker” (q), and accordingly in many of the cases the plaintiff has been a young man who has only just attained twenty-one (r). Mere inadequacy of price will, at any rate in such a case, entitle an expectant heir to apply to the Court to set aside the sale of a reversion, and the onus of proving the transaction fair, and the price sufficient, is on the purchaser (s).

The strength of these considerations is increased if the plaintiff is under the influence, however acquired, of the other party, and “no influence can be more direct, more intelligible, or more to be guarded against than that of a person who gets hold of a young man of fortune, and takes upon himself to supply him with means, pandering to his gross extravagance during his minority, and extorting from him, or at least obtaining from him, for every advance that he has made, a promise that the moment he comes of age it shall all be ratified so as to make the securities good” (t).

(p) Aylesford v. Morris, 8 Ch. 484, 492.

(s) O’Rorke v. Bolingbroke.

(t) Per Lord Cranworth, Smith v. Kay, 7 H. L. C. 750, 771.

### --- Page 0235 ---

Nature of the relation juridically.

PART II.  
PARENT AND CHILD.

——

CHAPTER VII.  
Section 1.—Rights of the Father over his Children.

“It is to be observed that in the laws of England there are three manner of guardianship; viz., by common law, by statute law, and by custom. By the common law there are four manner of guardians, viz., guardian in chivalry, guardian by nature, as the father of the eldest son, guardian in socage, and guardian per cause de nurture” (a). Though from this enumeration it will be seen that the relation between parent and child is in a legal point of view only a particular case of guardian and ward, yet, as this relationship has several distinguishing particularities, it will be more convenient to consider it apart.

The law of parent and child falls then under and is included in three of the heads of guardianship mentioned above, viz., guardianship by nature, by nurture, and by statute: the last, which arises under 4 & 5 Ph. & M. c. 8, repealed and re-enacted by 24 & 25 Vict. c. 100, s. 55, must be distinguished from the guardianship given by 12 Chas. II. c. 24, the chief remaining effect, as far as civil rights are concerned, of the former Statute being, as we shall see (b), to enlarge the duration of

(a) Co. Litt. 88 b.  
(b) Post, s. 4.

<br>

### --- Page 0236 ---

RIGHTS OF FATHER. 119

legal control by a father over his female children from fourteen to sixteen.

“According to the strict language of our law only an heir apparent can be the subject of guardianship by nature. Therefore when guardianship by nature is extended to children in general, or to any besides such as are heirs apparent, it is not conformable to the legal sense of the term among us, but must be understood to have reference to some rule independent of the common law. Thus when in Chancery the father and mother are styled the natural guardians of all their children born in marriage, or of any of their illegitimate issue, we should suppose those who express themselves so generally to refer to that sort of guardianship which the order and course of nature, so far as we are able to collect it by the light of reason, seem to point out, and to mean, that it is a good rule to regulate the guardianship by where positive law is silent, and it is in the discretion of the Lord Chancellor to settle the guardianship” (c). “As to guardianship by nurture, it only occurs where the infant is without any other guardian, and none can have it except the father or mother. It extends no further than the custody and government of the infant’s person, and determines at fourteen in the case both of males and females” (d).

Although guardianship by nature in its strict and technical sense is limited, as above mentioned, the Court of Chancery has in numerous cases emphatically recognized the father’s right as guardian by nature and nurture to the custody of his children (e); and the Courts of common law laid down the same rule (f).

(c) Co. Litt. 88 b, Harg. note 12.

(d) Ibid., note 13.

(e) Exp. Hopkins, 3 P. W. 152; Stileman v. Ashdown, 2 Atk. 477, 480; De Manneville v. De Manneville, 10 Ves. 62; Re Marquis of Salisbury, 2 Ch. D. p. 81; Agar-Ellis v. Lascelles, 10 V. & B. 49; 24 W. R. 817.

(f) Exp. Skinner, 9 Moore, 278; R. v. De Manneville, 5 East, 221;

<br>

### --- Page 0237 ---

120 RIGHTS OF FATHER

The right to their custody carried with it the right to determine all questions as to their education and religious training.

As against the mother, this right of the father, except as limited by statute, whether he keeps the children under his own control, or places them with a third person, is an absolute one even against the mother, though the child be an infant at the breast.

The Court cannot control the father’s common-law right, except as authorized by statute, or on the grounds hereafter discussed; nor had it even jurisdiction to let the mother have access to the child.

If the father agrees to let the mother have access, he has been held bound to do so, even at law.

The father’s right to the control and custody of his children, being good against the mother, is of course good against third parties, and they have no right to interfere. He may delegate part of his parental authority during his life to the tutor or schoolmaster of his child; who is then in loco parentis, and has such a portion of the power of the parent committed to him.

Re Hakewill, 12 C. B. 223; R. v. Greenhill, 4 A. & E. 624; Re Rare, Reg. v. Clark, 7 El. & Bl. 186; see the remarks of Patteson, J., p. 199; R. v. Howes, 3 El. & El. 332; Cartlidge v. Cartlidge, 2 Sw. & Tr. 567.

Agar-Ellis v. Lascelles, 10 C. D. 49; and see post, p. 130.

OVER INFANT CHILDREN. 121

charge, viz, that of constraint and correction, as may be necessary to answer the purposes for which he is employed.

From the extent of the father’s rights, it almost necessarily follows that children acquire by birth their father’s domicil, and they cannot change it by any acts of their own during infancy. The law relating to an infant’s domicil is fully discussed by Mr. Dicey in his text-book on that subject. After formulating the general rule that “the domicil of every dependent person is the same as, and changes (if at all) with, the domicil of the person on whom he is, as regards his domicil, legally dependent,” he thus states the rules as to infants, subject to certain exceptions mentioned below:—

(1) The domicil of a legitimate or legitimated infant is, during the lifetime of his father, the same as, and changes with, the domicil of his father. 

(2) The domicil of an illegitimate infant or of an infant whose father is dead, is, during the lifetime of the infant's mother, the same as, and changes with, the domicil of the mother.

(3) The domicil of an infant, without living parents, or of an illegitimate infant without a living mother,

(p) Stephens’ Black. 9th ed., ii, 296; Re Suttor, 2 F. & F. 267; Fitzgerald v. Northcote, 4 ibid. 656. As to the father’s power to chastise a child, see 1 Hawk. P. C. 130.

(q) Sommerville v. Sommerville, 5 Ves. 750, 787; Forbes v. Forbes, Kay, 341, 353; Jopp v. Wood 4 DeG. J. & S. 616. See also Dolphin v. Robins, 29 L. J. P. & M. 11; Yelverton v. Yelverton, ibid. 34; Re Daly, 25 Beav. 456, cases of married women; and Urquhart v. Butterfield, 37 C. D. 357.

(r) Dicey on Domicil, Pt. I., ch. 1, ii., B.

(s) Somerville v. Sommerville; Sharpe v. Crispin, LR. 1 P. & D, 611, 617; Forbes v. Forbes; Story, Conf. Laws, s. 46.

(t) Potinger v. Wightman, 3 Mer. 67; in America, Holyoke v. Hoskins, 5 Pick. 20; School Directors v. James, 2 Watts & Sargent, 567; Ryall v. Kennedy, 40 N. Y. (Sup. Court) 347, 361; and in Scotland, Arnott v. Groom, 1846, 9 D. 142.

possibly is the same as, and changes with, the domicil of his guardian (2) (u).

Exceptions. The exceptions to these rules are (1) that an infant’s domicil is not changed by the marriage of his mother (2), and (2) that the change of an infant’s home by a mother or guardian does not, if made with a fraudulent purpose (e.g., to affect the distribution of his estate, in case of his death), change his domicil (y).

Status. The status of an infant (z) must be determined by the law of his domicil; if legitimate there, he must be considered so here (a); but to make out his title to real estate in this country, he must not only show that he has the status of eldest legitimate son of his father, but that he is “heir” according to the lex loci rei sitae (b).

Control by a child’s marriage. There are two principal Marriage Acts now in force, the provisions of which it is not intended to discuss, except in so far as they bear upon the rights of infants. By the first of these (c), before a marriage can be celebrated, the banns must be published, or a licence obtained from the proper ecclesiastical authority.

(u) Sharpe v. Crispin, L. R., 1 P. & D. 611. The learned author goes on to say, that it will probably be held (in accordance with the rule on the Continent), that a guardian cannot change the domicil of his ward, and almost certainly that he cannot do this, unless the ward’s residence is, as a matter of fact, that of the guardian, citing a dictum of Wickens, V.-C., in Douglas v. Douglas, 12 Eq. p. 625. See also Story, Conf. Laws, ss. 505, 506, n. 1.

(z) Peasmar (xv) See in America, Ryall v. Kennedy, 4 N.Y. (Sup. Court) 347. A woman, of course, acquires her husband’s domicil. Dicey, n.

(y) See Potinger v. Wightman, 8 Mer. 67; but it is stated that this exception is open to doubt.

(z) This includes the capacity of a wife to bind herself by marriage-contract, Cooper v. Cooper, 13 App. Cas. 88.

(b) Birtwhistle v. Vardill, 7 Cl. & Fin. 895; Munro v. Munro, 7 ib. 842, and see He Don’s Estate, 4 Drew, 194; and Atkinson v. Anderson, 21 C. D. 100.

on

OVER INFANT CHILDREN,

s. 8 of the Act, if the parents or guardians whose consent is required, or one of them, at the time of the publication of the banns, publicly express dissent, such publication is void. By s. 14, before a licence can be obtained, if either of the parties, not being a widower or a widow, is under the age of twenty-one, one of the parties must swear that the proper consent has been obtained. By s. 16 the consent required is that of the father, if living, or, if he be dead, that of the lawfully-appointed guardians, or one of them; if there be no such guardians, then that of the mother, if unmarried, if not, the guardians of the person appointed by the Court, or one of them. And such consent is required for the marriage, unless there be no person who can consent. Though this consent is required, a marriage without it is a valid one; but if a person procures a marriage by a false oath or fraud, where one of the parties is an infant, he may be made to forfeit all the property accruing from the marriage. After a lapse of time the consent will be presumed to have been given, unless it be disproved.

By s. 17 it is provided, that if the father be non compos, or the guardians or mother be non compos, or beyond seas, or shall unreasonably refuse to consent to the marriage, either of the parties may apply by petition to the Court, and the Court may proceed upon such petition in a summary way, and if the proposed marriage appears to be proper, may judicially declare it to be so; and such judicial declaration is to be as effectual as a consent by the proper persons.

The consent need not be formal, Smith v. Huson, 1 Phill. 297; Hodgkinson v. Wilkie, 1 Hagg. Cons. 265; and may be retracted before the marriage, Hodgkinson v. Wilkie; Yonge v. Furse, 26 L. J. Ch. 117, 352: but retraction must be clear, and must be reasonable; see Strange v. Smith, Amb. 263.

R. v. Birmingham, 8 B.& C. 29; R. v. Clark, 2 Cox C. C. 183.

Ss. 23 & 24, post, ch. xix.

Harrison v. Southampton, 22 L. J. Ch. N. 8. 722.

<br>

### --- Page 0241 ---

Marriage by licence, &c.

The father's consent must be honestly given.

RIGHTS OF FATHER

It will be noticed that the only case in which a father’s consent can be supplied by the Court of Chancery is when he is non compos; the other contingencies are applicable only to guardians or mothers. Where a father beyond seas consented, and then died before the marriage, the infant was allowed to marry without any reference to the master. If the consent of the Court be obtained under this section on the faith of a proposal by the intended husband, the father being non compos, the settlement proposed to be made cannot be evaded by marrying by banns or otherwise without a licence.

By the second Marriage Act, amended by various subsequent statutes, two new modes of celebrating a marriage are sanctioned, viz., by the registrar's certificate, either with or without licence. By s. 10 of the principal Act, if the marriage is by certificate with licence, the same consent is required as would have been required to a marriage by licence before the Act; and whether the marriage be with or without licence, every person whose consent is required to a marriage with licence may forbid the issue of the registrar's certificate.

In some cases, as we have seen, the father’s consent to a child’s marriage is required; and in all cases the parental position and influence make his consent a matter of importance to a child. The Courts of Equity have therefore for a long time held that such consent must be honestly given, and if the parent or guardian stipulate for any private advantage from the marriage such an agreement shall be void, and any security given.

<br>

### --- Page 0242 ---

OVER INFANT CHILDREN.

To effect it shall be set aside (x); or (which is the same thing) if the consent is only given on having an underhand agreement by the child or one of the parties to the marriage to give back what the father apparently gives, such an agreement is a fraud upon the settlement, and will be set aside (o); and such an agreement may be set aside by one who is a party to the fraud, for it is against public policy to allow it to stand (p), unless setting it aside would be a fraud on an innocent party (q).

As grandfathers and grandmothers are bound by law to maintain their legitimate grandchildren (r), all persons have an interest in procuring a de facto marriage of their children to be declared a nullity, and can sustain a suit for that purpose (s); but after the death of a father who has brought such a suit, before decree, his executor cannot revive it, because the liability is merely personal (t). It was formerly held (u) that the mother could not revive it, as she had no interest when it commenced; but now that she is liable, to the extent of her separate estate (x), to maintain her grandchildren, it is submitted that she can now do so (y). In a suit for nullity instituted by the father of an infant, it must appear upon the face of the petition, whether he has instituted it in his own name, or as the infant’s guardian (z).

(n) Lamlee v. Hamman, 2 Vern. 499; Keat v. Allen, 2 Vern. 588; Hamilton v. Mohun, 2 Vern. 652.

### --- Page 0243 ---

126

Marriage brocage contracts void,

Power to appoint guardian,

Rights of the mother against the testamentary or other guardians,

RIGHTS OF FATHER

By a somewhat wider application of the same principle, all contracts or agreements to pay a reward to a person who promotes a marriage, usually known as marriage brocage contracts, are void (a), and cannot be made good by confirmation (d).

By 12 Car. II. c. 24, s. 8, where any person has any child under twenty-one years old and unmarried at the time of his death, the father of such child, whether born at his decease or in ventre sa mére, or whether such father be within twenty-one or of full age, may by deed executed or by last will in writing executed in the presence of two witnesses, dispose of the custody and tuition of such children until they attain twenty-one years, which disposition shall be good against all persons claiming the custody or tuition of such children, as guardian in socage or otherwise. By the Wills Act (c) the power of making a will is taken from infants, and an infant therefore can now only appoint guardians for his children by deed. The rights duties and powers of testamentary guardians will be discussed hereafter (d).

Section 2.—Rights of the Mother after the Father's Death.

We have seen (e) that the father’s right at common law to the control and custody of his children is an absolute one as against the mother. The cases in

(a) Talby, Potter, 3 Lev. 411; Stribblehill v. Brett, 2 Vern. 445; Goldsmith v. Brunning, 1 Eq. Cas. Abr. 89, sub nom. Smith v. Brunning, 2 Vern. 392; Drury v. Hook, 1 Vern. 412; Williamson v. Gihon, 2 Sch. & Lef. 357.

(b) Cole v. Gibson, 1 Ves. sen. 503.

(c) 1 Vict. c. 26 s. 7.

(d) Pest, ch. xi. s. 4.

(e) Ante, p. 120.

<br>

### --- Page 0244 ---

AND MOTHER.

which the Court is enabled to interfere with that right, either under its original jurisdiction or by statute, will be discussed hereafter: we have therefore only to consider the mother’s rights after the death of the father. These have been materially increased by a recent Statute (f). Under the old law, if the father appointed testamentary guardians, the mother had no right to interfere with them (g). In a well-known case (h), Lord Cottenham thus stated the law: “When this case was before me in the autumn, I had considerable reason to believe that there was much misapprehension in the mind of the mother as to her rights as mother, and I thought it necessary to explain that in point of law she had no right to control the power of the testamentary guardian. It is proper that mothers of children thus circumstanced should know that they have no right, as such, to interfere with testamentary guardians” (i); and the case was the same if the guardians were appointed by the Court (j). But by the 2nd section of the recent Act, it is provided that on the death of the father of an infant, and, in case the father was dead on

(f) The Guardianship of Infants Act, 1886 (49 & 50 Vict. p 27). As to the procedure under the Act, see the Rules of Dec. 1887. Section 9 confers a concurrent jurisdiction on the County Court of the district in which any respondent to any application under the Act resides.

(g) Formerly, if no testamentary guardian was appointed by the father, the mother was regarded by Courts of Equity as guardian by nature and nurture, Brendes v. Brutes, 3 Atk. 619, 624; Roach v. Garvan, 1 Ves. sen. 157. We have seen (ante, p. 119), that the term guardian by nature was not used in its strict and technical sense, but the mother is guardian by nurture, and has a right to the care and custody of her children within the age of nurture, and the care of their education, Villareal v. Mellish, 2 Sw. 533; Mellish v. Da Costa, 2 Atk. 14; at law as well as in equity, R. v. Clarke, 7 E. & B. 186; Re Moore, 11 Ir. Com. L. 1; Re Matthews, 12 Ir. Com. L. 233.

(h) Talbot v. Shrewsbury, 4 My. & Cr. 672, 683.

(i) Reynolds v. Teynham, 9 Med. 40, 4 B. P. C. 302.

(j) See Waine v. Waine, M. R. 1 Aug. 1839, Chamb. Infants, p. 36, where a mother, having been allowed to have her children during the holidays, refused to let them return to school, and it was held a gross contempt; Arnott v. Blessdale, 4 Sim. 387.

128 RIGHTS OF MOTHER

Guardianship the 25th of June, 1886, then from that date, the mother shall be the guardian (4), either alone when no guardian has been appointed by the father, or jointly with any guardian appointed by the father. When no guardian has been appointed by the father, or if the guardians or guardian appointed by him be dead, or refuse to act, the Court may, if it shall think fit, from time to time appoint a guardian or guardians to act jointly with the mother (1). And s. 4 gives every guardian in England and Ireland under the Act the same powers over the estate and person of the infant as those of guardians appointed under 12 Car. II. c. 24, and the corresponding Irish Statute (14 and 15 Car. II. c. 19) or otherwise (m).

When mother as the Statute 12 Car. II. c. 24 did not extend the power of appointing testamentary guardians to the mother, before the recent Act (n) any such appointment by her was void (0), though it was sometimes of practical use (p). But by s. 3 (1) of the Act of 1886, the mother may by deed or will (g) appoint any person or persons to be guardian or guardians of her infant children after the death of herself and the father (if such infant be then unmarried); and where guardians are appointed by both parents, they are to act jointly. By s. 3 (2) she may in like manner provisionally nominate some fit person or persons to act as guardian or guardians after her death jointly with the father, and the Court, after her death, if satisfied that he is

(4) She has no greater powers as regards the children’s religious education than those of any other guardian; Re Scanlan, 40 C. D. 200.

(1) In Re Scanlan, two Protestant guardians were thus appointed to act with the Roman Catholic mother, and the children were directed to be brought up as Protestants.

(m) See Re Scanlan.

(n) The Guardianship of Infants Act, 1886.

(o) Villareal v. Mellish, 2 Sw. 533; Exp. Edwards, 3 Atk. 519; Hunt v. Hunt, 2 Con. & L. 373.

(p) Re Kaye, 1 Ch. 387.

(g) But not by will whilst an infant; 1 Vict. c. 26, s. 7.

### --- Page 0246 ---

OVER INFANT CHILDREN. 129

For any reason unfitted to be the sole guardian, may confirm the appointment, or make such other order in a reasonable respect of the guardianship as it shall think fit. By s. 3 (3) if guardians are unable to agree upon a question affecting an infant’s welfare any of them may apply to the Court for its direction (r). The powers of all such guardians are defined by s. 4 (s). The Court may remove them, under s. 6, in its discretion, on being satisfied that it is for the infant’s welfare (t).

The mother, after being appointed guardian, was removable for misconduct, e.g., trying to bring about an improper marriage with her child, a ward of court (u), or if she was an unfit person (v), or if her moral character was very bad (y), or if she refused to obey the directions of the Court of Chancery as to the education of her children (z); and it is presumed that any reason sufficient for the interference of the Court with the exercise of his powers by a testamentary guardian (a) will be sufficient in the case of the mother, acting as guardian by virtue of the Act of 1886 (b).

It may now be regarded as a settled rule that, except under special circumstances, the child must be brought up in the father’s religion (c), even though he has died.

(r) See the Rules of Dec. 1887, made under this Act.  
(s) Ante, p. 128.  
(t) Under this section, the mother, resident in England, was, with her consent, removed, and a paternal uncle substituted; Re Lemons, 19 L. R. Ir. 575.  
(u) Roach v. Garvan, 1 Ves. sen. 158.  
(v) Heynsham v. Heynsham, 1 Cox, 179; see Re Besant, 11 C. D. 508.  
(y) Re Moore, 11 Ir. Com. L. 1.  
(z) Re Newbery, 1 Eq. 431; 1 Ch. 263.

130 RIGHTS OF MOTHER

intestate, and left no directions as to the religion in which he would wish the child to be educated (d). And at any rate, against the father’s special directions, the Court would be very reluctant to interfere on account of mere pecuniary benefit to the child. “If the Court were ever to exercise that discretion, it would be very difficult to say what was to be the extent of pecuniary benefit which should require the Court’s interference—what was to be the price of the child’s faith. It would be fraught with extreme danger” (e). The same reasoning would seem to apply if the father leave no directions, for his right is to have his children brought up in his own religion (f).

Father cannot bind himself This right the father cannot release, “nor can he bind himself conclusively by contract to exercise, in all events, in a particular way, rights which the law gives him for the benefit of his children and not for his own” (g). Consequently, an agreement by the husband with his wife (even though it is made before marriage, and the marriage but for such agreement would not have taken place), that the children, or some of them, shall be brought up in a religion different from their father’s is not legally binding (h), but such an agreement may be of practical weight after the father’s death (i).

Remark on the rule. It is too late now for any power short of the Legislature.

(d) Re North; Austin v. Austin; Hawksworth v. Hawksworth; see also Skinner v. Orde, L. R. 4 P. C. 60; Seton, 748, 751.

(e) Talbot v. Shrewsbury, 4 My. & Cr. p. 688.

(f) In a case where infants, interested in real estate in England, whose father was dead, were living with the mother out of the jurisdiction, she being one of three testamentary guardians, an order was made, at the instance of the other guardians, declaring that they ought to be educated in their father's religion, which was not that of the mother; Re Montagu, 28 C. D. 82.

(g) Andres v. Salt, 8 Ch. p. 636.

(h) Re Browne, 2 Ir. Ch. R. 151; Re Meades, Ir. R. 5 Eq. 985; Andrews v. Salt, 8 Ch. 622; Agar-Ellis v. Lascelles, 10 Ch. D. 49; D'Alton v. D’Alton, 4 P. D. 87.

(i) Post, p. 134.

### --- Page 0248 ---

OVER INFANT CHILDREN.

lature to alter a rule which, as we have seen, a long line of cases has settled, and which has been fully recognized in the Poor Law Acts, that a child must be educated in the religion of its deceased father; nor is the rule affected by the Guardianship of Infants Act, 1886, or by the Poor Law Act, 1889. If both parents are dead, such a rule may be a fitting one, but it seems a strange extension of the father’s rights when he is in his grave, to allow even his expressed wishes in such a case, and still more his merely presumed wishes, to override the rights of the living parent. The result in many cases, to quote the forcible remarks of Wickens, V.-C., is “to create a barrier between a widowed mother and her only child, to annul the mother’s influence over her daughter on the most important of all subjects, with the almost inevitable effect of weakening it on all others; to introduce a disturbing element into a union which ought to be as close, as warm, and as absolute as any known to man; and lastly, to inflict severe pain on both mother and child.”

Although a mother is bound to bring up her children in their father’s religion, it does not necessarily follow that they are to be removed from her custody, if she is of a different faith. In spite of some of the older cases, when the penal laws against the Catholics were in force, this may now be regarded as settled.

In certain special cases the father by his conduct lost his rights by waiver.

<br>

### --- Page 0249 ---

132

RIGHTS OF MOTHER

Before his death has been held to have waived his right that the children after his death shall be brought up in his religion. No definite rule can be given as to what conduct will amount to a waiver on his part: it is a question of fact, and will vary according to the infinite diversity of facts. The cases, however, will give a general idea of the kind of conduct that will constitute a waiver. Thus in Hill v. Hill (q), the father, a Roman Catholic, died leaving two daughters, aged respectively seven and two years, whom he directed to be brought up as Roman Catholics, and appointed a Mr. Proctor, a Roman Catholic, and his (the testator’s) wife, a Protestant, to be their guardians. There had been some discussion previous to the marriage as to an agreement that the boys of the family should be brought up as Roman Catholics, and the girls as Protestants, but Wood, V.-C., held that there was no proof that any actual agreement had been come to. The eldest child was privately baptized by a Catholic priest in the mother’s absence, but was rebaptized in a Protestant chapel in the testator’s presence. The child was never taken to any Roman Catholic place of worship, nor did the father in any way exercise any control over the religious instruction of the child, and when she was six had a Protestant governess for her. The second child was baptized openly in the Church of England. The Court held that the father’s right was waived by his conduct (r). In Re O’Malleys (s) the father was a Roman Catholic and the mother a Protestant. The children were baptized as Roman Catholics, but the father allowed the mother to bring them up as Protestants. He died when the eldest child was eleven years.

(q) 81 L. J. Ch. 505.

(r) See also the similar cases, Re Garnet, 20 W. R. 222; Re Clarke, 16 L. R. Ir. 817, where the Court was also influenced by the consideration "that it would be for the infant's benefit to be brought up with his sister in the mother's faith"; and Re Walsh, 13 L. R. Ir. 239.

(s) 8 Ir. Ch. R. 172; see also Re Grimes, 11 Ib. 465.

OVER INFANT CHILDREN.

old, and the mother had the custody of them for a year, when she died. There was contradictory evidence as to a declaration made by the father on his death-bed, that he wished the children to be brought up as Roman Catholics. They were ordered to be left with the maternal aunt and brought up as Protestants. So in a very similar case (t) the father was a Roman Catholic, though apparently little more than a nominal one. The children had been baptized as Roman Catholics, but brought up by the mother as Protestants; and she had been appointed by her husband guardian. The ages of the children were eleven and ten. They were ordered to be brought up as Protestants.

In Re Meades (wu) an ante-nuptial engagement had been made that the daughters should be brought up as Roman Catholics; the father was a Protestant, and the mother a Roman Catholic. The two daughters were baptized as Roman Catholics, and brought up in that faith till the mother’s death, when they were aged respectively seven and six; their instruction in that religion was continued till they attained ten and nine respectively, and then the father took them to live with them, and for nine months educated them as Protestants. The Lord Chancellor of Ireland refused to interfere with the father’s right to educate them as Protestants. It is to be observed, however, in that case that the father was living, and it would require much stronger circumstances to justify the Court’s interference during the father’s life than if he were dead (x). In the recent case of Andrews v. Salt (y) the father was a Roman Catholic and the mother a Protestant, and there had been a verbal agreement before marriage that the girls should be brought up as Protestants and

Effect of agreement by father to have children brought up in different religion,

RIGHTS OF MOTHER

the boys as Catholics. The father allowed the child in question, a girl, to be baptized as a Protestant, though he apparently disapproved of it. He died when the child was nine months old, leaving no property to support his children. The guardian, who was directed to bring up the children as Roman Catholics, allowed the girl to remain with the mother and her family, and to be brought up by them as a Protestant, till she was nine. He then claimed the custody of her, and long discussions took place for two years, during which she continued with the mother. The Lords Justices, though of the opinion that the child had not so far imbibed the distinctive doctrines of the Church of England that it would be cruel or dangerous on that ground only to have her educated as a Roman Catholic, held the father’s right had been in fact waived by his conduct and that of the guardian. So where an Englishwoman, the mother of illegitimate children, agreed with the father, a Mohammedan to whom, though he was already married, she was married in England according to the Mohammedan ritual, that they should live separate, and the children should reside with him, and be brought up in his religion, it was held after his death, four years afterwards, that they ought to be left in the custody of guardians appointed by him, she having no absolute right to their custody, and that being most for their benefit (2).

Though, as we have seen, an agreement that any of the children of the marriage shall be brought up in a religion different from that of the father is not legally binding, yet “if, after the death of the father, circumstances happen which in the opinion of the Court make it for the benefit of the child to be educated in the religion of the mother, and the question arises whether the father had so acted that he ought to be held in this Court to have waived or abandoned his right to

(2) Re Ullee, 54 L. T. 286,

<br>

### --- Page 0252 ---

OVER INFANT CHILDREN. 135

have his child educated in his own religion, the fact that the father before marriage promised the mother that girls, the issue of the marriage, should be educated in her religion, is a circumstance to which in our opinion weight and perhaps great weight ought to be attached (a).

Although the father may not by his conduct have waived his right to have his child brought up in his own religion, yet if the child be in fact trained for some time in a different one, so that it would be dangerous and cruel to it to enforce a change, no change will be made; and the judge may, if he think fit, have an interview with the child to ascertain whether the fact is so (b). The last cited was a strong case, for the Court had ordered the child to be brought up as a Roman Catholic, and the guardian, a Protestant, in contempt of the order, took her to the Continent and brought her up as a Protestant till the age of fifteen.

The Lord Chancellor of Ireland, after an interview with the child, refused to make any change.

It is obvious that a rule of this kind is open to great abuse, as it may lead “widowed mothers, in breach of their duty and to the great injury of their children, to introduce them prematurely into an atmosphere of theological controversy, than which nothing can be more injurious to the mind and heart of a child, and this it will be observed for the purpose of convincing” them that their dead father had wrong views” (c).

Accordingly the Court will be reluctant to follow Stourton v. Stourton, unless “the impressions produced on a child’s mind are considered to be so great and so permanent as to induce the Court to fear lest any attempt at altering them would do more harm than

Section 3,—Illegitimate Children.

No legal In the eye of the law an illegitimate child is filius nullius,
guardian, and consequently has no legal guardians, not even the mother or the putative father (g). Though the mother has no legal right to the possession of the child, yet if it be within the age of nurture, and the putative father take it out of the mother’s possession by force or fraud, even a Court of law on habeas corpus directed it to be delivered to the mother (h);

and the Court will prefer the mother to the father (i); but there is no case deciding that, if the father had the custody fairly, the Court would deprive him of it (k).

While the child is within the age of nurture it seems doubtful whether the father has any right to take it from the custody of any one with whom it may be (l).

But in a very recent case (m) it was held that the mother has a natural right to its custody, which will be regarded by the Court; and, after her death, the putative father will be preferred to her relatives (n).

It is sometimes a matter of doubt, whether a child born in wedlock is legitimate or not (o). In one case a husband was allowed to obtain a decree in a suit, nominally to administer a fund settled by him on the children of the marriage, but in reality to obtain a declaration that the youngest child was illegitimate (p).

This case has been disapproved of, and at any rate such a suit could only be maintained by a parent (q).

Practice under the Act, when petitioner an infant,

ILLEGITIMATE INFANT CHILDREN,

in England, may apply by petition to the Probate Division of the Court, praying the Court for a decree declaring that the petitioner is the legitimate child of his parents, and that the marriage of his father and mother or of his grandfather and grandmother was a valid marriage, or for a decree declaring either of the matters aforesaid; and any such subject or person, being so domiciled or claiming as aforesaid, may in like manner apply to the Court for a decree declaring that his marriage was or is a valid marriage, and the Court has jurisdiction to hear and determine such application and to make such decree declaratory of the legitimacy or illegitimacy of such person, or of the validity or invalidity of such marriage, as to the Court may seem just; and such decree, except as hereinafter mentioned, is binding to all intents and purposes on Her Majesty and on all persons whomsoever.

The Court may require persons to be cited (q), and the rights of persons who have not been cited are saved (r).

The object of the Act is to enable an infant to obtain a declaration of legitimacy, not to enable others to prove him to be illegitimate (s); the Court will therefore direct a reference, whether the proceedings are for the infant’s benefit, and if the object be to prove him to be illegitimate, will not allow them (t). The Court has no jurisdiction under the Act to decide upon a claim to any title of honour, as a baronetcy (u).

The petition must be by guardian (x), who must not have any interest in the infant being declared illegitimate (y).

(q) S. 7; see as to the practice in this particular, Mansel v. Attorney-General, 4 P. D. 232; Brinkley v. Attorney-General, 14 ib. 83.

### --- Page 0256 ---

ENFORCING FATHER’S RIGHTS. 139

Section 4—How a Father's Rights over his Children are Enforced.

(1) At Law, before the Judicature Acts.

The father’s right to the custody of his children might be directly enforced in two ways, by the writ of habeas corpus, and by application to the Court of Chancery. It was possible also to proceed by the writ de homine replegiando, which is addressed to the sheriff, and orders him to replevy a man out of custody in the same way that chattels taken in distress may be replevied; but this method of procedure has long ago fallen into disuse.

The writ of habeas corpus is a writ issuing out of the High Court of Justice, and is directed to any person who detains another in custody, ordering him to produce the body of such person, with the day and cause of his caption and detention. It may be taken out by the father, or the person to whom the father, if abroad, has given the custody (2).

A Court of Common Law could only have regard to the legal right of the father; it had (except in extreme cases) no discretionary power to control the father in the exercise of his rights, such as the Court of Chancery representing the sovereign possessed.

Accordingly on habeas corpus, a child which had not arrived at years of discretion was necessarily delivered up to the proper custody, i.e. the father, if living, or other guardian (a); and the father might sue out this writ without making any previous demand for possession of the child (b). The child was ordered to be delivered to the father, though of bad character (c), or

(2) Re Suttor, 2 Fost. & F. 267.  
(a) R. v. Greenhill, 4 A. & E. 624; Exp. Hakewill, 12 C. B. 223; R. v. Clarke, 7 E. & B. 186; Reg. v. Howes, 3 E. & E. 332.  
(b) Exp. Witte, 18 C. B. 680.  
(c) Exp. Skinner, 9 Moore, 278.

Exception in cases of gross immorality.

though there were circumstances which would have induced the Court of Chancery to hold that he had waived his right to the custody(<).

In Re Preston (e) a habeas corpus was refused to the legal guardian, who was in that case the mother, apparently on the ground that she had waived her right by acquiescing in his being under other custody; but the true ground of the decision was that the mother, being in India, made the application by power of attorney, so that the infant could not be delivered to her (f).

Courts of Law had, however, a discretion to refuse to deliver an infant to the father, if they thought he would abuse that right to the injury of the child(g). “There is an admitted qualification of the right of the father or guardian if he be grossly immoral, or if he wishes to have the child for any unlawful purpose” (h). This discretion was exercised in several of the older cases, though the imperfect nature of the reports does not bring out the true reason of the decision very clearly(i). But the immorality must be very gross; drunkenness, and profligacy was not sufficient(k); nor these combined with destitution(l); nor the fact that the father was living in adultery, if he did not bring the child into

ENFORCING FATHER’S RIGHTS.

contact with the woman. In Re Moore, on a habeas corpus taken out by the mother, who was the guardian, the Court was equally divided as to whether she had forfeited her right by gross and deliberate lying; she having obtained the benefits of a Protestant institution for her children by swearing that she and they were Protestants, when they were in fact Roman Catholics. In a recent case, a mother was held to have forfeited her right to the custody of a child of ten, by having led a drunken and immoral life, and left the child to starve.

We have seen that if the infant had not reached years of discretion it would be delivered to the father, or proper legal guardian, without examination, but “if the child be able to exercise a choice, the Court leaves him to elect where he will go.” “The right to such election, it has now been clearly decided, depends on age alone, and not on mental capacity.” There is no case in England expressly determining the age at which a male infant acquires such right of election, but it might be gathered from R. v. Clarke that it is fourteen, at which time the age of nurture ceases.

In Ireland there are two cases expressly deciding this point. In Re Shanahan, a father took out a habeas corpus to recover possession of his son, an idiot, who was at an institution. The boy was proved to be over fourteen, but under sixteen, and the Judges having had an interview with him, and found that he had capacity to exercise a choice, refused to deliver him to his father against his wishes. This case was followed in Re.

<br>

### --- Page 0259 ---

142 ENFORCING FATHER’S RIGHTS.

Connor (t), where, after much discussion, the Court refused on a habeas corpus by the father to deliver to him a boy between fourteen and sixteen.

Over female infants to sixteen. In the case of girls, guided partly by the language of 4 and 5 Ph. and M.c. 8(u), which implies that the custody of girls under sixteen belongs to their parents, the Queen’s Bench decided that a girl under sixteen has not the right of electing where she will live (w).

What sufficient answer . . we to writ of culpus to recover possession of a child, it is not a habeas corpus. If the father, or a widowed mother, take out a habeas corpus writ, it is not a sufficient answer by the person to whom the writ is addressed, that the child was not, at the time of issuing the writ, or at any time since, under his custody or control, or any one employed by him. He should go on to state whether he knows where the child is, and by whom he was taken (x); nor is it any excuse, that, after wrongfully detaining the child, he handed it over to another person before the issue of the writ (y).

Actions for many of the rights which arise from the relationship of master and servant arise also in the case of parent and child. Thus a father may bring an action to recover damages for loss of service arising from personal injuries done to a child by assault, battery, negligence, &c., or by a child being enticed away from home (z), or in the case of a daughter being seduced, but there must be some actual loss of service or implied service. If the

(t) 16 Ir. Com. L. 112; where O’Brien, J., dissented, relying on a dictum of Patteson, J., and thought the father’s legal right to the custody of his children extended to twenty-one.

(u) Repealed and re-enacted by 24 & 25 Vict. c. 100, s. 55; see post, p. 213.

(w) Rv. Howes, 3 F. & E. 332, followed by the Probate Court, Mallinson v. Mallinson, L. R. 1 P. & D. 221.

(x) Rv. Roberts, 2 F. & F. 272, and Re Matthews, 12 Tr. Com. L. 233, in which 2. v. Roberts is more fully stated.

### --- Page 0260 ---

ENFORCING FATHER'S RIGHTS. 143

child be too young to render service, an action of the kind will not lie (a); but if the child be capable of performing service, it will in many cases be implied from the fact of the relationship (b).

The father could formerly maintain no action if the injury in question caused the child’s death; and the child had no right of action for injuries done to his parent. The law was amended to a certain extent in both these points by Lord Campbell’s Act (c), which enacts that an action shall be maintainable notwithstanding the death of the party injured (d). Every such action is to be for the benefit of the wife, husband, parent, and child of the person whose death shall have been so caused, and to be brought by and in the name of the executor or administrator of the person deceased; and in every such action the jury may give such damages as they may think proportioned to the injury resulting from such death, to the parties respectively for whom and for whose benefit the action is brought; and the amount so recovered, after deducting the costs not recovered from the defendant, is to be divided amongst the before-mentioned parties in such shares as the jury by their verdict shall find and direct (e).

An illegitimate child is not within the Act (f). The illegitimate children not within Act, jury are not entitled, in assessing damages, to take into account the feelings of the survivors as distinct from their pecuniary loss; the damage must be actual pecuniary damage (g).

(a) Hall v. Hollander, 4 B. & C. 660.

(b) For a fuller account the reader is referred to Smith on Master and Servant, c. 8, § 2; Addison on Torts, 5th ed. 531.

(c) 9 & 10 Vict. c. 93.

(d) § 1.

(e) § 2; see Bulmer v. Bulmer, 25 C. D. 409, where money paid to the executors by a railway company, no action having been brought, was apportioned by the Court.

(f) Dickinson v. N. E. Rail. Co., 33 L. J. Exch. 91.

(g) Duckworth v. Johnson, 4 H. & N. 653. For a fuller discussion on the Act, see Addison on Torts, 5th ed. ch. viii. § 4.
