(u) Burton v. Earl of Darnley, 8 Eq. 576.

(z) Ransbotham v. Senior, 8 Eq. 575, mentioned with approval in Heath v. Crealock, 15 Eq. 257.

(y) Rosenberg v. Lindo, 48 L. T. 478.

(2) Rochford v. Hackman, Kay, 309; Todd v. Lynes, ante, p. 145.

450

Application as to education, &c.

Abduction of female infants.

CRIMINAL LAW.

allow him to go at liberty (a), or may exercise its discretion by allowing the ward to remain where he is, if there are special circumstances, as where he had enlisted, and declared that if he were discharged he would enlist again the next day (b); or where a girl of twenty expressed her strong disinclination to return to her home (c).

When an infant is a ward of Court, his education and bringing up is under the direct control of the Court, and if the guardians have any difficulty they should apply in chambers for directions (d).

Since the Judicature Acts came into operation, the relief given by the Chancery Division is, as we have seen (e), usually resorted to.

Section 3.—Criminal Statutes.

We may here notice that by 24 & 25 Vict. c. 100, s. 53, it is a felony fraudulently to abduct any woman under the age of twenty-one, who has any interest in any real or personal estate, or is a presumptive heiress or next of kin to any person having such interest, out of the possession and against the will of her father or mother, or any other person having the lawful care or charge of her, with intent to marry her, &c., and the offender may be punished with penal servitude, and if convicted, is incapable of taking any interest in the woman’s property, and if a marriage has taken place,

(a) Bond v. Roberts, 13 Sim. 400. See the order made, given from the Reg. Lib., Kay, 310, n.

(b) Harrison v. Goodall, Kay, 310, n.

(c) Re Lyons, 22 L. T. N. S. 770.

(d) Kay v. Johnston, 21 Beav. 538; and see post, p. 453, as to the control exercised by the Court over guardians.

(e) Ante, p. 144.

<br>

### --- Page 0568 ---

CRIMINAL LAW.

Her property is to be settled in such manner as the Court shall direct, upon information at the suit of the Attorney-General.

By s. 55 it is a misdemeanor punishable with two years’ imprisonment to abduct any unmarried girl under the age of sixteen out of the possession and against the will of her father or mother, or of any other person having the lawful care or charge of her, whether the abductor knows her age or not, and though he bona fide believed, on reasonable grounds, that she was over that age (f).

By s. 56 it is a felony punishable with penal servitude, unlawfully by force or fraud to take away or detain (g) any child under the age of fourteen years with intent to deprive any parent, guardian, or other person having the lawful care or charge of such child of the possession of such child; but no person who has claimed any right to the possession of such child, or is the mother, or has claimed to be the father of an illegitimate child, shall be liable to be prosecuted under the Act, on account of the getting possession of such child, or taking such child out of the possession of any person having the lawful charge thereof.

Under s. 1 of the Criminal Law Amendment Act, 1885 (48 & 49 Vict. c. 69), the procuring or attempting to procure any woman or girl to become a prostitute is a misdemeanor punishable with two years’ imprisonment; and defiling a girl under the age of thirteen is a felony, punishable with penal servitude for life (s. 4); if she be between thirteen and sixteen it is a misdemeanor, punishable with two years’ imprisonment, but it is a sufficient defence that the person charged had reasonable cause to believe that she was of or above the age of sixteen. Section 6 imposes similar penalties on

(f) Reg. v. Prince, L. R. 2 C. C. R. 154; secus under the Act of 1885, supra.

(g) Reg. v. Johnson, 50 L. T. 759.

<br>

### --- Page 0569 ---

CRIMINAL LAW.

Householders inducing, or knowingly suffering any girl to resort to or be in his premises for the purpose of prostitution. Under s. 7 any person who with intent that any unmarried girl under eighteen shall be defiled takes her or causes her to be taken out of the possession and against the will of her father or mother, or any other person having the lawful care or charge of her, is guilty of a misdemeanor, punishable as aforesaid, but it is a sufficient defence that the person charged had reasonable cause to believe that she was of or above that age. Unlawful detention of any woman or girl for the like purposes is a misdemeanor, punishable as aforesaid. We have seen that under s. 12 any parent, guardian, master, or mistress, causing, encouraging, or favouring the seduction or prostitution of any girl under sixteen may be divested of all authority, and

a guardian may be appointed.

(hk) Ante, p. 165.

### --- Page 0570 ---

CHAPTER XXVII.  
REMEDIES AGAINST GUARDIANS.  
Section 1—At Law, before the Judicature Acts.

With regard to the person, a Court of law might interfere upon habeas corpus, if the infant were ill-treated, but it would require a strong case to induce an interference with the legal right of the guardian to the custody (a).

As regards property, the infant had three remedies, all of which are now abolished or obsolete; (1.) by action of waste, (2.) by assize of novel disseisin, and (3.) action of account, of which the last alone is still existent, though it has practically fallen into disuse, and the account is always now taken in the Chancery Division, where the Court can have regard to allowances such as maintenance, which a jury could not notice.

Section 2.—In Chancery, and since the Judicature Acts (b), in the High Court.

First, as regards the person. As to the person,

There is no doubt of the full jurisdiction of the Court over guardians of every kind, whether socage or testa- guardians,

(a) See ante, p. 140.  
(b) See ante, p. 144.

<br>

### --- Page 0571 ---

454 REMEDIES  
mentary (b); or of whatever sort (c). The better opinion seems to be that the Court has no power actually to remove a testamentary guardian (d). However this may be, there is no doubt of the power of the Court to supersede a legal guardian from acting, and appoint someone to take charge of the infant in his place, and perhaps this is all that is meant where it is said (e) that the Court has jurisdiction to remove testamentary guardians, if they act improperly.

Difference There is a difference between testamentary guardians appointed by the Court. “Although the appointed by Court has the power of interfering in certain cases with testamentary guardians, it proceeds on very different rules and principles from those which regulate its conduct where the discretion of appointing guardians devolves upon it in the first instance” (f). The control of these guardians is not taken away by the infants being made wards of Court (g), and the Court will not interfere unless it be really necessary for the infants’ advantage (h).

What grounds The ground of interference is of course the benefit of of interfe- the infant. Thus a testamentary guardian will be rence suffi- sufficient superseded as being an unfit person, if he be a lunatic (i).

Garvan, 1 Ves. sen. 160; Eyre v. Shaftesbury, 2 P. AGAINST GUARDIANS. 455

or bankrupt (8). The second marriage of a woman does not disqualify her; but a reference will generally be made to inquire into the circumstances, and under this she may be reappointed (J). The guardians being trustees cannot resign their office merely because they wish it (m); but their unwillingness to act may be a good reason for appointing somebody else, on the application of the infant (n). Absence from the jurisdiction is not in itself a sufficient reason for supersession (o).

The Court will also interfere in any case of misconduct. Benefit of infant

Thus Lord Hardwicke said, “If any wrong steps had been taken which might not deserve punishment, yet if they were such as induced the least suspicion of the infant's being like to suffer by the conduct of the guardians, or if the guardians chose to make use of methods that might turn to the prejudice of the infant, the Court would interpose and order the contrary” (p). A guardian has been superseded for permitting an unfitting marriage (q); for tampering with the ward’s religious views (r); for allowing his own rights to lapse by acquiescence (s); and see the cases (t) in which the Court interferes with a father’s rights.

To supersede a legal guardian on the ground of misconduct, an action must be brought: it will not be done except in an action on petition, unless the guardian consent (u).

Another reason for interference is when the guardians Difference of view between guardians.

REMEDIES

differ among themselves as to the management of their ward, as to the school to which he should be sent (2), or the person with whom he should reside (y), or the method of education generally (z); in such a case a reference to approve of a scheme for his maintenance and education cannot be dispensed with (a).

(2.) As to property.

Guardians are, as we have seen, trustees, and the Court will, in exercise of its ordinary jurisdiction, see that the trust is properly exercised, and punish a breach of it (b). If there is any suspected mismanagement of the property, any one may apply on behalf of the infant (c). An action at law for an account could not be brought while the guardianship continued, but in equity a guardian might always have been made to account during minority (d).

To prevent guardians and trustees of infants, and induce other persons having an interest pur autre vie from continuing in possession of land after the death of the infant, or cestui que vie, it is by 6 Anne, c. 18, s. 1, enacted that any person claiming an estate in remainder, reversion, or expectancy in or to any estate after the death of any infant or other such person, on affidavit that he has cause to believe such infant or other person to be dead, and that his death is concealed by such guardian, trustee, or any other person, may, once a year, if he thinks fit, move for an order on the guardian, trustee, or other person suspected of concealing such death, to produce the person supposed to be dead before the parties named in the order; and on non-compliance

### --- Page 0574 ---

AGAINST GUARDIANS.

With this order, may obtain an order to have the person supposed to be dead produced in the Court or before Commissioners; and on non-production, of which a return is to be made and filed, the person so concealed is to be taken to be dead, and the claimant may enter on his lands. By s. 2, on affidavit that the minor is beyond sea, the claimant may send over persons to view the minor. By s. 3, if it afterwards appears that the infant was alive at the date of the order, he or his guardians may re-enter and recover the rents and profits. By s. 4, if the guardians or trustees make it appear to the satisfaction of the Court, that they cannot procure the production of the infant, but that he is living at the date of the return, they may continue in possession and receipt of the rents and profits. By s. 5, if the guardians or trustees of an infant hold over after his death, the person entitled on the death of such infant may recover from them the full value of the profits received during such wrongful possession (e).

The costs of producing the infant, or cestui que vie fall upon the guardian or tenant pur autre vie; they cannot be thrown on the applicant (f). A person entitled on the death of an infant or cestui que vie, subject to an intermediate contingent remainder, may apply (g); and a person entitled under an executory devise is a person claiming “in expectancy” (h). The order may be made upon a person having an interest determinable on a life, though not strictly a tenant pur autre vie (i); and on an assignee of a tenant for life (k). For the form of the order for production, see the cases mentioned below (l). If the person in possession make

### --- Page 0576 ---

CHAPTER XXVIII.

TERMINATION OF GUARDIANSHIP.

Section 1.—Settling Accounts and Release of Guardian.

GUARDIANSHIP (other than guardianship in socage, which terminates when the infant is fourteen), ceases to be when the ward attains twenty-one. If the trusteeship is then brought to a close, the accounts should be settled, and the guardian and trustee is entitled to an acknowledgment that all claims and demands have been settled.

In practice a release is usually given, but it seems this cannot be insisted on as a matter of strict right (a). In Ward after twenty-one the Court must order that such release or acknowledgment of account have full information on the part of the ward of the circumstances, and what his rights are (b), and without any undue pressure (c).

It is regarded as a suspicious circumstance if the accounts are settled, or a release given, a few days after the ward has attained twenty-one (d); and in all such cases (e) the release will be set aside, unless the trustee can show that the ward fully understood the accounts (f), and, if

(d) Steadman v. Pulling, 3 Atk. 423; Hicks v. Hicks, 3 Atk. 274.

(e) Cartwright v. Western, Sel. Ch. Cas. 84; Mylch v. Packington, 3 B. P.C. 46.

Section 2.—How far Ward’s Rights bound by Acquiescence and Lapse of Time.

Lapse of time no bar, while trustee, and therefore the Statute of Limitations does not apply to accounts between them (m); as long as the relation continues, lapse of time is no bar (n), and the account may be taken from the commencement on the footing of guardian and ward (o). Nor is the law altered by the Trustee Act, 1888; for the provisions under which the Statutes of Limitation may be pleaded do not apply where the claim against the trustee is founded upon any fraud or fraudulent breach of trust to which he was party or privy, or is to recover trust property retained by him, or received by him and converted to his use (p). During infancy, the ward cannot in any

### --- Page 0578 ---

OF GUARDIANSHIP. 461

way authorize a breach of trust, but he may be bound by acquiescence after attaining twenty-one. Acquiescence for five years in a release would prima facie be a bar, and after a long lapse of time the Court would not set aside a release merely on proof of errors, but the nature of the errors would have considerable effect in determining whether the release had been fairly obtained. Thus an account was not disturbed, though settled soon after the cestui que trust attained twenty-one, after being acquiesced in for twenty years, and assignments by a minor to his tutor have been upheld after many years, the minor having had dealings with the tutor which amounted to a release. Acquiescence is no bar while the relation of trustee and cestui que trust or the influence caused by it continues, or the circumstances remain unaltered under which the transaction in question took place. There must be full knowledge on the part of the person who waives his rights by acquiescence or by confirmation, and if the original act be fraudulent, the Court will scrutinize with great jealousy any alleged confirmation of it. Acquiescence is said by Mr. Lewin to be of two kinds; first, direct, where the act complained of is done.

462 GIFTS

with the full knowledge and approbation of another, in which case he cannot complain of an act to which he was in fact a party; and secondly, indirect, where a person having a right to set aside a transaction stands by and sees another dealing with property in a manner inconsistent with that right, and makes no objection. To this latter head may be referred the well-known rule of the Courts of Equity, that delay in suing, unless accounted for, will, in certain cases, disentitle the plaintiff to relief; or, in other words, the Court discourages stale demands, as circumstances may have altered, and the accounting party may be unable to prove his payments. The exact length of time which will be a bar must of course depend on the circumstances of the case; nearly forty years has been held sufficient, twenty years, ten years.

Section 8.—Gifts by Ward to Guardian.

Onus rests on closely connected with this subject is that of the guardian to receipt of benefits by the guardian from his ward. In show no undue influence. Lord Eldon’s words, “a guardian cannot take anything from his ward pending the guardianship, or at the close of it, or at any period until his influence has ceased to exist.” This rule does not absolutely exclude a guardian from taking a benefit from his ward, but it

TO GUARDIANS.

throws upon the guardian the onus of proving that there was no undue influence, but that everything was voluntary and fair, with full warning and due knowledge. The subject of confidential relations of this kind was much considered by Lord Brougham in his elaborate judgment in Hunter v. Atkins (1). He said: “There are certain relations known to the law, as attorney, guardian, trustee. If a person standing in these relations to client, ward, or cestui que trust takes a gift or makes a bargain, the proof lies upon him that he has dealt with the other party, the client, ward, &c., exactly as a stranger would have done, taking no advantage of his influence or knowledge, putting the other party on his guard, bringing everything to his knowledge which he himself knew. In short, the rule rightly considered is, that the person standing in such relation must, before he can take a gift or even enter into a transaction, place himself exactly in the same position as a stranger would have been in, so that he may gain no advantage from his relation to the other party beyond what may be the natural and unavoidable consequence of kindness arising out of that relation.” (1)

The rule is placed by Lord Hardwicke on grounds of public utility. He says: “Where a man acts as guardian or trustee in the nature of guardian for an infant, the Court is extremely watchful to prevent that person taking any advantage upon his ward or cestui que trust coming of age, and at the time of settling the account or delivering up the trust; because an undue advantage may be taken. It would give an opportunity either by flattery or force, by good usage unfairly meant, or by bad usage imposed, to take such advantage; and therefore the principle of the Court is of the same nature with relief in this Court on the head of public utility, as

(1) 3 M. & K. 185,
(2) See, however, the remarks of Lord Romilly on this case, in Cooke v. Lamotte, 15 Beav. 241.

463

<br>

### --- Page 0581 ---

464

GIFTS

in bonds obtained from young heirs, and rewards given to an attorney pending a cause, and marriage brocage bonds. All depend upon public utility; and therefore the Court will not suffer it, though perhaps in a particular instance there may not be an actual unfairness. “The rule of the Court as to guardians is extremely strict, and in some cases does infer some hardship; as where there has been a great deal of trouble and he has acted fairly and honestly, that yet he shall have no allowance; but the Court has established that on great utility, and on necessity, and on this principle of humanity, it is a debt of humanity that one man owes to another, as every man is liable to be in the same circumstances. Undoubtedly, if after the ward or cestui que trust comes of age, and after being actually put into possession of the estate, he thinks fit, when sui juris and at liberty, to grant that or any other reasonable grant by way of reward for care and trouble, when done with eyes open, the Court could never set that aside; but the Court guards against doing it at the very time of accounting and delivering up the estate, as the terms; for the Court will not suffer them to make that the terms of doing their duty.” In that case (m) the gift of an annuity to the guardian by the ward soon after attaining twenty-one, the accounts being settled at the same time, was set aside. A leading case on this subject is that of Hatch v. Hatch (n). The plaintiff, Mrs. Hatch, had lived with her brother-in-law and guardian, Giles Hatch, since she was four years old. Soon after attaining twenty-one in 1779, she conveyed an advowson to him, “in consideration of her great friendship, kindness and regard for him.” She continued to live with him till 1784, when she married his brother, Thomas Hatch, an attorney, who had prepared the conveyance of the advowson. In 1800, after

(m) Hylton v. Hylton, 2 Ves. sen. 547.  
(n) 9 Ves. 292.

<br>

### --- Page 0582 ---

TO GUARDIANS. 465

The death of Giles, the plaintiff and her husband filed their bill to set aside the conveyance. Lord Eldon held that if the bill had been filed in 1785, the guardian could not have retained the benefit of the deed, and as the husband had in fact been a party to the transaction, the subsequent lapse of time was not a bar. With regard to the general rule he said (o): “This case proves the wisdom of the Court in saying, it is almost impossible in the course of the connexion of guardian and ward, attorney and client, trustee and cestui que trust, that a transaction shall stand, purporting to be bounty for the execution of antecedent duty. There may not be a more moral act, one that would do more credit to a young man beginning the world, or afford a better omen for the future, than if, a trustee having done his duty, the cestui que trust, taking it into his fair, serious, and well-informed consideration, were to do an act of bounty like this, But the Court cannot permit it, except quite satisfied that the act is of that nature, for the reason often given; and recollecting that in discussing whether it is an act of rational consideration, an act of pure volition, uninfluenced, that inquiry is so easily baffled in a Court of justice, that instead of the spontaneous act of a friend, uninfluenced, it may be the impulse of a mind misled by undue kindness or forced by oppression; the difficulty of getting property out of the hands of the guardian or trustee thus increased; and therefore if the Court does not watch these transactions with a jealousy almost invincible, in a great majority of cases it will lend its assistance to fraud, where the connexion is not dissolved, the account not settled, everything remaining pressing upon the mind of the party under the care of the guardian or trustee.” This rule has often been acted on, and gifts made to a guardian (7), or

GIFTS

conveyances (q), or leases (r), made for an undervalue to the guardian, or some member of his family (s), have been set aside, and this after a considerable lapse of time. Confirmation will not render the gift valid, if made under the continuing influence of the guardian (t). The rule has been extended to persons who occupy a confidential relationship, though not formally guardians (u), e.g., servant who had the care of the infant (x), a religious community with whom the infant lived during minority (y), a brother-in-law with whom the infant lived (z); but in a recent case (a), a gift to a relative with whom the infant, aged twenty, had lived four months commencing from the time of her father’s death, made only a month before her own death, was held not invalid.

A third person (other than a bona fide purchaser without notice) (b) cannot take advantage of a gift obtained by undue influence. Thus, in Maitland v. Irving (c), two creditors consented to postpone payment of a debt of 500l. due to them from Maclean, in consideration of his giving them the guarantee of the plaintiff, his niece and former ward, who lived with him, and had attained twenty-one a year and a half previously. A promissory note for 1200l., and a cheque by the plaintiff for 3000l., were afterwards substituted for the guarantee. On the cheque being dishonoured the creditors brought an action for the 3000l, and the

### --- Page 0584 ---

TO GUARDIANS.

Plaintiff filed her bill to restrain them from recovering at law. The defendants, the creditors, knew that Maclean could not perform his contract, and that the plaintiff was a young lady who was residing with him, and was possessed of a large fortune, and to whom he had been guardian. It was held by Shadwell, V.-C., that in these circumstances it was incumbent on them to inquire whether the young lady was a free agent, and that they could stand in no better situation than Maclean; and the injunction was accordingly granted.

A somewhat similar case was that of Muitland v. Backhouse (d), also arising out of a transaction between Maclean and his ward. He induced her to indorse a promissory note for 5000l., drawn by him in her favour; the note was given to a third person, who paid it into his bankers, they being also bankers of Maclean. The bankers knew that Maclean was in embarrassed circumstances, and were cognizant of the position in which the plaintiff stood towards him; nevertheless, without further inquiry, on the faith of the indorsement they discounted the note. The Court held that the bankers were affected by the equities between Maclean and his ward, and restrained them from suing on the note.

This decision was affirmed by Lord Cottenham (e). In several other cases the Court has set aside voluntary securities given by a ward soon after attaining twenty-one to a creditor of the guardian, or quasi guardian, where the creditor knew, or might have known, of the relation between the parties (f), even after the lapse of several years, if the influence in question has continued, and the creditor has taken no steps to enforce his security (g).

(f) Archer v. Hudson, 7 Beav. 551; Espey v. Lake, 10 W.R. 269; Dettmar v. Metropolitan Bank, H. & M. 641; Serconie v. Sanders, 34 Beav. 382.

(g) Kempson v. Ashbee, 10 Ch. 15. See also ante, p. 184.

### --- Page 0585 ---

Application must be by next friend,

PART IV.

PROCEDURE,

CHAPTER XXIX.

ACTIONS (4) AND APPLICATIONS TO THE COURT BY INFANTS (6).

Section 1—Commencement of Proceedings.

It is no longer necessary to consider separately the procedure of Courts of law and equity in actions and other applications to the Court by infants. It is provided by the Rules of the Supreme Court, 1883, Ord. XVI, r. 16, that infants may sue as plaintiffs by their next friends, in the manner theretofore practised in the Chancery Division.

The law considers that an infant is incapable of asserting or protecting his rights, or forming a judgment as to the necessity of applying for protection or redress to the tribunals of the country, and he is unable to make himself liable to the defendant for the costs; it therefore requires every application on an infant's behalf to be supported by a next friend, who may be responsible to the Court that the application is not a

(a) An “action” is a civil proceeding commenced by writ or in such other manner as may be prescribed by Rules of Court; Jud. Act, 1873, s. 100. It includes proceedings commenced by originating summons; Galland v. Burton, 20 C. D. 231.

(6) Dan, Ch. Pr. 104–116.

<br>

### --- Page 0586 ---

ACTIONS BY INFANTS. 469

wanton and improper one, and to the defendant or respondent for the costs. This rule applies to actions (c), petitions (d), and motions (e), and all other applications. The application may be on behalf of infants in ventre sa mere (f). It is that of the infants by their next friend, not of the next friend as an individual (g); and the infant is described in the title to the action or matter as “A. B., an infant, by C. D., of, &c., his next friend” (h). If an action be commenced without a next friend, the defendant may move for it to be dismissed, with costs to be paid by the solicitor who filed it (i); but his proper course would appear to be to apply by summons on notice to stay proceedings, until a next friend be appointed (k).

The next friend is so called because the nearest relation would be the person who would naturally come forward to assert the infant’s rights, and, unless the interest of the father or the nearest paternal relative is adverse to that of the infant, he is the proper person to be the next friend (l). But as the nearest relation may be supine in the matter, or be the very person who has infringed those rights, any person, even a stranger, may apply on an infant’s behalf (m); in such a case, however, the Court will weigh carefully, on the one hand, the danger of encouraging useless and expensive litigation; and on the other, of discouraging interference, which is very often absolutely necessary for an infant’s

(c) Flight v. Bolland, 4 Russ. 298, 301.

(d) Jones v. Lewis, 1 De G. & S. 245; Re Russell, 15 Jur. 981.

(e) Cox v. Wright, 9 Jur. N. S. 981.

(f) Wallis v. Hodson, 2 Atk. 117; Musgrave v. Parry, 2 Vern. 710; Robinson v. Litton, 3 Atk. 209, 211.

(g) Pidduck v. Bowlthce, 2 Sim. N. S. 223.

(h) Burney’s Forms, pp. 24 (21), 106.

(i) Flight v. Bolland.

(k) Dan. Ch. Pr. 117; and see Exp. Brocklebank, 6 C. D. p. 860.

(m) Andrews v. Cradock, Prec. Ch. 376; Pomfret v. Windsor, 2 Ves. sen. 484.

### --- Page 0587 ---

470 ACTIONS

Protection (n). The office of next friend is distinct from that of guardian (0), though the same person may fill both characters. 

Who disqualifies the next friend. The next friend should not be a defendant (p), unless he be a mere formal party (9), or a friend of a defendant, accepting the office at the defendant’s request, for there ought not, either in form or substance, to be the same person both plaintiff and defendant (7); nor should he be a person whose interest may in any way conflict with that of the infant (s). Before the name of any person may be used in any action as next friend of any infant, such person must sign a written authority to the solicitor for that purpose, and the authority is to be filed in the Central Office, or in the District Registry, if the cause or matter is proceeding therein (f). If the name of a next friend be used without his consent, it will on his application be struck out (uw). The Court always expects a next friend to be a person of substance (z); but except in the case of an appeal by an insolvent next friend (y), it does not require the next friend of an infant to give security for costs, as formerly in the case of a married woman (z); the reason being that the Court is anxious not to discourage proceedings on behalf of infants, while it has jurisdiction to stay them, if improper (a). If the same next friend acts for a feme

(0) Harris v. Lightfoot, 10 W. R. 31.

(p) Pame v. Little, 13 Beav. 114; Anon., 11 Jur. 258; Lewis v. Nobbs, 8 C. D. 591.

(gy) Taylor v. Taylor, W. N. 1881, 81.

(r) Per Cotton, L. J., Burgess v. Bottomley, 25 C. D. 246.

(8) Jacob v. Lucas, 1 Beav. 436.

### --- Page 0588 ---

BY INFANTS.

Coverte and infant plaintiffs, the action may be stayed till security is given. A feme coverte suing in respect of her separate estate does not now require a next friend, and cannot be required to give security for costs; but a feme coverte cannot be the next friend. An infant cannot sue by a next friend in forma pauperis, unless under special circumstances.

If there be any suspicion that the action is an improper one, or the next friend is unfit to have the conduct of it, an inquiry may be directed on these points. It is said there must be a strong case to justify such an inquiry; but it has been directed, where the next friend was a solicitor not in any way connected with the family, and where the suit was instituted without any communication with the family, the next friend being the son of the plaintiff's solicitor. 

“The true and the just principle which should govern all such cases is this. No discouragement ought to be thrown in the way of persons bona fide suing as next friends; but no undue facility should be given to mere volunteers, who interfere rather for their own purposes than for the infant’s advantage. While they appear to act bona fide they will be protected; the presumption will rather be in their favour; the proof will rather be thrown on those who impeach their motives; the leaning will be more for than against them. But no strained presumptions will be made to protect them.

(6) Drinan v. Manniz, 3 Dru. & War. 154; and see Greenaway v. Kotheram, 9 Sim. 88.

(c) See the Married Women’s Property Act, 1882, sec. 1 (2); Jacob v. Isaac, 30 C. D. 418.

(d) Thynne v. St. Maur, 84 C. D. 465.

### --- Page 0589 ---

Similar inquiry in administration action by infant.

Inquiry how directed.

ACTIONS

no forced constructions will be put upon their conduct; no benefit from bare possibilities will be conjured up on their behalf. They must be content to have their motives appreciated, and their acts judged like other parties. If they have involved themselves in suspicion, their proceedings must be subjected to inquiry; if they have incurred just blame, be it by improper interference, or be it by unnecessary interference, they must abide the consequences; the suit at their instance must be stayed; or if the suit be useful to the infant, but the parties instituting it be unfit to conduct it, they must give place to others in whom the Court can better repose confidence.

In the exercise of the discretion now given to the Court to grant or refuse an order for the administration of any trust or of the estate of any deceased person, the Court will in a proper case, where an infant is plaintiff, direct an inquiry, whether such an order should be made or not.

The inquiry may be directed on petition; but the modern practice is to apply to the Court upon motion, of which notice is given to the next friend. It is in the discretion of the judge whether the inquiry shall be directed, and the exercise of his discretion will not be interfered with by the Court of Appeal. The Probate Division has a similar power, where a will is disputed by a guardian ad litem on behalf of infants, to inquire whether the suit is for their benefit. If it appear that the action is not for the infant's benefit,

<br>

### --- Page 0590 ---

BY INFANTS. 473

it will be stayed (x); or if the circumstances warrant it, dismissed with costs to be paid by the next friend(s); and this will be done in a clear case without any inquiry being directed (c). If it be shown to be beneficial, the defendant, at whose instance the inquiry is made, will have to pay the costs of the application (u), and an action apparently beneficial will not be stopped merely on allegation of improper motives (x). The costs of such an inquiry are entirely in the discretion of the Court (y). The inquiry will not be directed at the instance of the next friend, as by instituting the suit he undertakes that it is beneficial (z); and it is too late to ask for it at the hearing (a), or after judgment (b).

When the property of an infant is the subject of proceedings already pending, and it becomes necessary that others should be instituted on his behalf, before any steps are taken, an order should be obtained at chambers on summons, sanctioning such contemplated proceedings as being for the benefit of the infant (c).

The next friend cannot be receiver in a cause (d), and could not formerly give evidence (e). The latter disability has now been removed (f).

Section 2.—Change of next Friend.

A next friend will be removed, if he have an interest in the suit adverse to the infant (g); or be an accounting party in the suit (h); or be so closely connected with defendants having an adverse interest, that the infant’s interest will probably not be protected (i); or if he will not proceed with the cause (k), or conducts it improperly (l), or refuses to appeal, when desired so to do (m). In the absence of a special reason to the contrary, the father (x), or testamentary guardian (o), may apply at any time to be substituted as the next friend in a subsisting action. But a next friend will not be removed

What grounds are insufﬁcient.

merely on the ground of poverty (p), nor on grounds of suspicion, with no substantial case against his solvency, character, or conduct (q), nor merely because he is connected with the defendants, as by being clerk to their solicitor (r), if there is no probability that the infant’s interest will be injuriously affected thereby (s), even if he be a stranger to the infant (t). Where the solicitor of the next friend had incurred unnecessary costs, and the next friend had been removed and the official solicitor substituted, the Court of Appeal discharged the solicitor.

order, as the Court had power to call on the next friend to change his solicitor, and power to deal with the solicitor’s costs.

A next friend cannot on his own motion withdraw without giving security for costs, if necessary, showing that the next friend to be appointed in his place is a proper and substantial person.

Where the next friend was a feme sole and married, it was held that the suit abated, and that the infant must get a new next friend, and obtain an order to revive; if he did not, the defendant was entitled to an order that the plaintiff revive, or that the bill be dismissed.

On the death of the next friend, the nearest paternal relations are the proper persons to nominate a new one. The application should be by ex parte motion, and no inquiry will be directed as to his fitness.

Where it becomes necessary to appoint a new next friend, application should be made by the solicitor of the plaintiff (in the name of some one as next friend for the purposes of the application) for an order appointing a new next friend. The application may be in Court or at chambers. After appointment the name of the new next friend should be made use of in all subsequent proceedings, where the former one, if alive, would have been named. Before the defendant

Leniency of Court of Chancery towards an infant plaintiff.

ACTIONS

has appeared, the name of the new next friend may be introduced into the record, under an order as of course to amend; and after appearance the same may be done, where the new next friend is appointed in the place of a deceased next friend, if the application for the order is made by the solicitor who acted for the deceased next friend; if not so made, an order to change solicitors must be obtained before the application to appoint the new next friend. In other cases the order may be obtained on the plaintiff's petition as of course, if the defendant’s solicitors subscribe their consent thereto, if not by motion or summons on notice. If the plaintiff's solicitor omits within a reasonable time to take steps to get a new next friend appointed, the defendant may apply to the Court, by motion or notice, for an order directing the approval of a new next friend, and for the insertion of his name as such in the proceedings. A new next friend has been appointed by the chief clerk’s certificate without further order (e). The order appointing the new next friend must in every case be served on the solicitors of the defendants, and be left for entry in the cause-books. Before appointing a new next friend, the Court requires to be satisfied of his willingness to act, and an authority signed by such next friend should be produced and filed (f).

Section 3.—Proceedings in the Action.

It has been said that the act of filing a bill by a next friend does not bind the infant (g), and that as the Court considers infants as particularly under its protection, it will not permit an infant plaintiff to be preju-

(e) Large v. De Ferre, Braith. Pr. 558.

diced by the manner in which his bill is framed (k). Under the old practice, when misjoinder of parties (l) or multifariousness (m) was fatal to an adult plaintiff's case, this doctrine was frequently of great advantage to an infant (l); but now that neither misjoinder (m) nor multifariousness (m) is a fatal objection, and the Court has in every case full powers of amendment (o), it is seldom, if ever, necessary to apply it.

There has been much difference of judicial opinion on the question whether a next friend or guardian ad litem of an infant or other person not sui juris can be required to give discovery; but the better opinion is that the Rules relating to discovery (p) do not apply, and that he cannot be compelled either to make an affidavit of documents (q) or answer interrogatories (r); nor will the action be stayed until discovery is given, as an indirect mode of effecting the same result (s).

An infant was not bound by a submission in his pleadings (t); and where an adult heir filed a bill and stated that his ancestor’s will was duly executed and attested, and died before the hearing, leaving an infant heir, it was held that the will must be proved, as the first heir might have dismissed his bill, and claimed

An infant cannot agree to submit a cause to arbitration (x), but such agreement is voidable only (a); and the Court may, under ss. 13, 14, of the Arbitration Act, 1889, refer any cause or matter or any question or issue of fact therein, if all the parties interested who are sui juris consent. It seems to have been at one time the practice to refer it to the Master whether it would be for the infant’s benefit that the cause should be submitted (2). Whatever validity there may be in an infant’s submission to arbitration, it is revoked by his death before award (c). After an award, it is too late for adult parties, who knew of the fact, to object that some of the parties were infants (d).

A writ of ne exeat regno may be obtained on an infant plaintiff's affidavit (k). The undertaking as to no great damages is entered into by the next friend signing the Registrar’s book (l). But his writ is now rarely issued (m).

Section 4.— Where more than one Action instituted.

If more than one action or proceeding is instituted in an infant’s name, which is not unfrequently the case, the Court will, before judgment in any of them, direct an inquiry, which is most beneficial to the infant (n).

If they are in the same branch of the Court, the inquiry will be directed on an ex parte motion, on the simple allegation that they are for the same purpose (o). If they are in different branches of the Court, an order must first be obtained for the transfer of one of them, so that they may be before the same judge, and the inquiry is then directed on special motion, of which notice must be given to the other parties (p).

480

Effect of inquiry being directed,

Where both proceedings proper,

ACTIONS

The order for the inquiry is made in both actions, and does not necessarily stay the proceedings, though it usually has that effect (p); either plaintiff may amend, and the amendment does not stop the reference (q), but it is irregular to obtain an order of course to amend (r).

When the result of the inquiry is certified, any application that may be necessary is made by motion, on notice (s).

If both the proceedings are proper, both may be allowed to go on (t). The next friend in the second “must show some defect in the first, or a decided preference in the second; if the merits are only equal, the priority must prevail” (w), and the proceedings in the second will be stayed. If the first, though beneficial, is less so than the second, proceedings in the first will be stayed, but the next friend will be allowed his costs (z). If the first is improper, it may be stayed (y), or dismissed with costs, to be paid by the next friend (2).

Where one of the causes is in the paper for hearing (a), or after judgment in one of them (b), it is not usual to direct such an inquiry, though it has been done (c), but proceedings in the others will be stayed, with liberty to the next friend in each of the stayed causes to apply for the conduct of the one in which the

### --- Page 0598 ---

BY INFANTS.

Judgment is pronounced, and it is entirely a question of the infant’s interest which next friend shall have the conduct.

Section 5.—If Infant Plaintiff attain Twenty-one during the proceedings.

If an infant sole plaintiff attain twenty-one during the proceedings, he may elect whether he will go on with them or not. The next friend should not take any further steps in his name, even though they are consequential on former proceedings. If the late infant elects to continue the action, the proceedings will be continued in his own name, and he will be liable for the costs from the beginning, as if he had been of age when it was commenced. The title will be altered, and will run: A. B., late an infant by C. D. his next friend, but now of full age, plaintiff.

If he elects to abandon the action, he may obtain an order on motion of course to dismiss, on payment of costs by himself, or he may refrain from taking any step at all. He cannot make the next friend pay the costs, unless the action was an improper one. If he refrains from taking any steps, the defendant may move to dismiss the action, but he cannot make the late infant pay the costs, even though the next friend is dead, so that no order can be made against him.

(d) Kenyon v. Kenyon, 35 Beav. 300.  
(e) Nanny v. Wynn, 2 Jur. 962; Frost v. Ward, 2 DeG. J. & S. 0.  
(f) Brown v. Weatherhead, 4 Ha. 122. But the next friend is dominus litis, till it is shown that the infant is of age: Almack v. Moore, 2 L. R. Ir. 90.

(h) Wyatt, P. R. 225.

### --- Page 0599 ---

Infant co-plaintiff repudiating.

Change of solicitor.

Costs of action dismissed.

Next friend liable as against the defendant,

ACTIONS BY INFANTS,

The repudiation relates back to the beginning of the action.

If an infant is co-plaintiff with others, and, on attaining twenty-one, wishes to repudiate the action, he should move on notice to have his name struck out as a plaintiff. This will be done, but if the next friend wish it, he will be made a defendant.

An infant co-plaintiff on coming of age cannot without a special application change his solicitor, or appear by different counsel, nor can the next friend obtain an order of course to change the solicitor.

Section 6.—Costs of Infant Plaintiff.

If an action brought on behalf of an infant is dismissed, the defendant is entitled to his costs, in the same way that he would be against an adult, and that even though the proceedings were commenced with the sanction of the Court.

As against the defendant the next friend is liable to pay the costs of an unsuccessful action or application, even though he was made next friend without his consent; but in the latter case he might recover.

(1) Dunn v. Dunn, 7 De G. M. & G. 25.

(n) Bicknell v. Bicknell, 32 Beav. 381.

(o) Swift v. Gracebrook, 13 Sim. 185.

(p) Ballard v. White, 2 Ha. 158.

(q) Brown v. Brown, 11 Beav. 562; see Hutchinson v. Norwood (No. 2), 84 W. R. 637.

(r) Frank v. Mainwaring, 4 Beav. 37.

(s) Bligh v. Tredgett, 5 De G. & S. 74.

(t) Jones v. Lewis, 1 De G. & S. 245; Howard v. Prince, 14 Beav. 28, n.

(u) Ward v. Ward, 6 Beav. 251.

### --- Page 0600 ---

COSTS. 483

against the solicitor who appointed him without authority (2).

Costs may often be charged on the real (y) or personal estate which is being administered (z), or the infant’s share of it (a); but costs ought not to be given out of the estate, except for those proceedings which are, in their origin, reasonably directed for the benefit of the estate, or have in their result conduced to that benefit (b). Accordingly, where in an administration suit charges of misconduct were made against the trustee, which were dismissed, the plaintiff could not recover costs out of the estate (c); but the Court has jurisdiction to order the trustee’s costs to be paid out of the estate, when the next friend was unable to pay them, and though the trustee, while defending the trust estate, was also defending himself from charges of fraud brought by the infant plaintiff (d); and apparently he could not recover against the infant personally (e).

The plaintiff’s solicitor may sue the next friend for his costs, even while the action is pending, if the next friend deny his liability (f).

Prima facie the next friend is entitled to recover costs against the infant (g), if the action be a proper one, or for such part of it as is proper (h), and this even if the next friend have in fact taken a mistaken view (i).

(z) Bligh v. Tredgett, 5 De G. & J. 74.  
(y) Mandeno v. Mandeno, Kay, App. 2.  
(2) Cross v. Cross, 8 Beav. 455; and see post, p. 485.  
(a) Mackenzie v. Taylor, 7 Beav. 467.  
(c) Bartlett v. Wood, 9 W. R. 817.  
(d) Ellis v. Ellis, 1 Russ. 368.  
(d) Walters v. Woodbridge, 7 C. D. 504.  
(e) Turner v. Turner, 2 Stra. 708.  
(f) Hawkes v. Cottrel, 3 H. & N. 243.

### --- Page 0601 ---

484 ACTIONS BY INFANTS.

so that it is dismissed (&). But if it is unnecessary or improper, and the next friend might, with reasonable care, have known it to be so, he must pay the costs personally (/) ; and the same rule applies to any improper application in an action (m).

Charges and expenses The next friend may be allowed charges and expenses under the head of just allowances, for if only the taxed costs were allowed, persons would deliberate before taking the office (7). When the infant's costs are ordered to be paid out of a fund in Court to which he is entitled in reversion, party and party costs only can be immediately paid, but the next friend will have liberty to apply for the difference between the party and party costs and those between solicitor and client, when the fund comes into possession (0), but there should not be any taxation as between solicitor and client till that time (p).

Death of party Formerly where costs were ordered to be paid by the former next friend, and the infant or next friend died before taxation, such costs were lost (g). There might be a revivor if the costs had been taxed (r), and now see the Attorneys and Solicitors Act, 1870 (s).

Matter of As between the infant and the next friend, the costs are entirely in the discretion of the Court (¢), but there

friend made to pay costs.

(k) Taner v. Ivic, 2 Ves. sen. 466.

(l) Pearce v. Pearce, 9 Ves, 548; Campbell v. Campbell, 2 My. & Cr. 25; Anderton v. Yates, 5 De G. & S. 202; Flight v. Bolland, 4 Russ. 298.

(m) Buckly v. Puckcridge, 1 Dick. 395; Roddam v. Hetherington, 5 Ves. 91, 95; Brown v. Brown, 11 Beav, 562.

(n) Fearns v. Young, 10 Ves. 184; and see Nelson v. Duncombe, 9 Beav. 211.

(o) Damant v. Hennell, 33 C. D, 224.

(p) Marshall v. Marshall, W. N. 1888, 82.

(g) Morgan v. Compton, Bunb. 332.

(r) Lowten v. Colchester, 2 Mer. 113.

(s) 33 & 34 Vict. c. 28, s. 19; Dan. Ch. Pr. 298; and Doggett v. Eastern Counties Railway, 6 Ch. 474.

(t) Smartwood v. Rutter, 9 Ha. 24, 27; Clayton v. Clarke, 7 Jur. N, 8. 562.

### --- Page 0602 ---

COSTS. 485

is sometimes difficulty in exercising the discretion so as not on the one hand to encourage unnecessary litigation, or on the other discourage interference, which is often necessary for the infant’s protection (u).

The power of the next friend, or the solicitor employed by him, to recover costs from the infant is aided by the lien the latter has on a fund realized in a cause for costs in that cause (2).

By the Attorneys and Solicitors Act, 1860 (y), a solicitor employed to prosecute or defend a suit or matter may have a charge for his costs on the property recovered or preserved, and after a declaration of charge, he has a charge upon and against and a right to payment out of the property, of whatsoever nature it may be. It was considered by Stuart, V.-C., that a solicitor could not be “employed” by an infant within the meaning of the Act (z), but this view is incorrect (a). If the infant adopt the suit after attaining twenty-one, the lien may be enforced against him then (b); if he repudiate the suit, the solicitor has no lien on deeds belonging to the infant, which were deposited by the defendant for inspection in the suit (c).

For cases where property was held to be “recovered or preserved” within the meaning of the Act, and for the whole subject of solicitor’s lien, see the authorities mentioned below (d).

(u) Cross v. Cross, 8 Beav. 455.

(x) Staines v. Maddox, Mos. 319; Pritchard v. Roberts, 17 Eq. 222.

(y) 28 & 24 Vict. c. 127, s. 28.

(z) Bonser v. Bradshaw, 30 L. J. Ch. 159.

(a) Re Keane, 12 Eq. 115, 123; Baile v. Baile, 13 Eq. 497; Greer v. Young, 24 C. D. 545.

(b) Bonser v. Bradshaw, 4 Giff. 260; Baile v. Baile.

(c) Dunn v. Dunn, 7 De G. M. & G. 25.

(d) Twynam v. Porter, 11 Eq. 181; Re Fiddey, 7 Ch. 773; Pinkerton v. Easton, 16 Eq. 490; Foxon v. Gascoigne, 9 Ch. 654; Dan. Ch. Pr. c. 87, s. 1; Cordery on Solicitors, 2nd ed. ch. xii.

### --- Page 0603 ---

Infant must defend by guardian.

CHAPTER XXX.  
ACTIONS AND APPLICATIONS AGAINST INFANTS.

Section 1.—Commencement of Proceedings.

Actions and other applications may be brought and made against infants; the plaintiff need not join any person with them as defendants, nor is it usual for him to describe them in the title of the action or other proceedings as infants, unless any question turns on the fact of their infancy, but after a guardian ad litem has been appointed, that is shown in the title by describing the infant as “A. B., an infant, by C. D. his guardian.” Where an infant, a necessary party to a suit, had not been baptized, and the parents delayed the baptism in order to put difficulties in the plaintiff's way, the infant was allowed to be described as the youngest daughter of her father and mother.

Infants are deemed incapable of defending themselves, and accordingly it is provided that they may, in the manner heretofore practised in the Chancery Division, defend by their guardians appointed for that purpose. This description of guardian is called the “guardian ad litem,” to distinguish him from the guardian of the estate or person. An infant married

(a) See ante, p. 468 (a).  
(b) Dan. Ch. Pr. 172–181.  
(c) Dan. Ch. Pr. 172.  
(d) See next page.  
(e) Dan. Forms, 3rd ed. 97.  
(f) Eley v. Broughton, 28 & St. 188.  
(g) R.S.C., Ord. XVI. r. 16.

<br>

### --- Page 0604 ---

ACTIONS AGAINST INFANTS. 487

woman is on the same footing as other infants in this respect (h).

Applications in the proceedings should be made by the guardian ad litem.

When an infant is a defendant to the action, service on his father or guardian, or if none, then upon the person with whom the infant resides, or under whose care he is, shall, unless the Court or a judge otherwise orders, be deemed good service on the infant; provided that the Court or judge may order that service made or to be made on the infant shall be deemed good service (i).

An infant shall not enter an appearance except by his guardian ad litem (j). No order for the appointment of such guardian shall be necessary, but the solicitor applying to enter such appearance shall make and file an affidavit in the form No. 8 in Appendix A., Part II, with such variations as circumstances may require (k). Every infant served with a petition or notice of motion, or summons in a matter, shall appear on the hearing thereof by a guardian ad litem in all cases in which the appointment of a special guardian is not provided for. No order for the appointment of such guardian shall be necessary, but the solicitor by whom he appears must previously make and file an affidavit as in the last rule mentioned (l).

Section 2.—Appointment of Guardian ad litem.

Where no appearance has been entered to a writ of summons for a defendant who is an infant, or a person not appear.

(h) Colman v. Northcote, 2 Ha. 147.  
(i) B.S.C. Ord. IX. 7.  
(j) Where a solicitor entered an appearance in the ordinary way, not knowing that the defendant was an infant, he was held not liable for costs thrown away by subsequent abortive proceedings; Wade v. Keefe, 22 L. R. Ir. 154.  
(k) Ib. Ord. XVI. r. 18; see next page.  
(l) Ib. r. 19.

### --- Page 0605 ---

488 ACTIONS AGAINST INFANTS,

of unsound mind not so found by inquisition, the plaintiff shall before further proceeding with the action against the defendant, apply to the Court or a judge for an order that some proper person be assigned guardian of such defendant, by whom he may appear and defend the action. But no such order shall be made unless it appears on the hearing of such application that the writ of summons was duly served, and that notice of such application was, after the expiration of the time allowed for appearance, and at least six clear days before the day in such notice named for hearing the application, served upon or left at the dwelling-house of the person with whom or under whose care such defendant was at the time of serving such writ of summons, and also (in the case of such defendant being an infant not residing with or under the care of his father or guardian) served upon or left at the dwelling-house of the father or guardian, if any, of such infant, unless the Court or judge at the time of hearing such application shall dispense with such last-mentioned service (m). The same course must be pursued, though the infant is out of the jurisdiction (n); but where the infant had no substantial interest, and the plaintiff had served him with copy of the bill, and at the same time given his father notice, that on default made in appearing he should apply for the appointment of a guardian ad litem, notice of the application was dispensed with (o). Sunday is reckoned as one of the six days mentioned in the order (p).

As we have seen (q), no order for the appointment of

Guardian how appointed. guardian is necessary. The form of affidavit required is as follows (r) :—

GUARDIAN AD LITEM. 489

“I, ____, of ____, make oath and say as follows:—

A. B., of ____, is a fit and proper person to act as guardian ad litem of the above-named infant defendant, and has no interest in the matters in question in this action adverse to that of the said infant, and the consent of the said A. B. to act as such guardian is hereto annexed” (s).

The application to appoint a guardian ad litem for an infant of unsound mind should be in the Chancery Division and not in Lunacy (t).

If the application is made at the instance of the infant, any suitable person may be chosen; if made by the plaintiff, the solicitor to the suitor’s fee fund is usually appointed (u).

The proposed guardian should be resident within the jurisdiction (z), other than the plaintiff, and not a married woman (y). The Court will prefer a competent person with no adverse interest, already a party to the suit, rather than a stranger (a); a relative or friend rather than a mere volunteer (b); thus the father may be appointed (c), the father-in-law (d), the testamentary guardian (e), or other guardian of the person (f).

On the death of a guardian ad litem a new one will be appointed.

(s) To this affidavit is to be annexed the document signed by such guardian in testimony of his consent to act.  
(t) Pidcock v. Boultbee, 2 De G. M. & G. 898; and see Re Brown, 1 Mac. & G. 201.

(z) Anon., 18 Jur. 770; Hartland v. Atcherley, 7 Beav. 53.  
(y) Dan. Ch. Pr. 173.  
(a) Anon., 9 Ha. App. 27; Bonfield v. Grant, 11 W. R. 275; Newman v. Selfe, 11 W. R. 765.  
(b) Foster v. Cautley, 10 Ha. App. 24.  
(c) Jongsma v. Pfiel, 9 Ves. 357; Fowler v. Ward, 6 Jur. 403.  
(d) Quarrell v. Binmore, 8 Jur. 1113.  
(e) Shuttleworth v. Shuttleworth, 2 Ha. 147.  
(f) Sandys v. Cooper, 4 L. J. Ch. N. S. 162.

490

Removal of guardian,  
Duty of guardian.  
Infant not bound by admissions in pleadings.

ACTIONS AGAINST INFANTS.

be appointed (f), for which a special application is required (g).

The guardian will be removed if he does not do his duty, or for other sufficient grounds (h), or if he has been improperly appointed (i).

Section 3.—Infant’s Defence.

The duty of the guardian is to properly defend the action for the infant. Under the old practice, when an answer was required from every defendant, a formal answer was put in, submitting the infant's rights to the protection of the Court.

It is often unnecessary, and if so, it is improper for an infant to put in a formal defence (k). Every allegation of fact in any pleading not being a petition or summons, if not denied specifically or by necessary implication, or stated to be not admitted in the pleading of the opposite party, is taken to be admitted, except as against an infant, lunatic or person of unsound mind (l); and therefore the plaintiff must prove his case. Nor can judgment be obtained on express admissions in the infant’s statement of defence (m). Where, however, judgment is signed

(f) Drant v. Vause, 2 Y. & C. C. C. 524; Needham v. Smith, 6 Beav. 130.  
(g) Burney’s Forms, p. 48.  
(h) Russell v. Sharpe, 1 J. & W. 482; Dan. Ch. Pr. 175.  
(i) Sandys v. Cooper, 4 L. J. Ch. N. S. 162.  
(k) Fitzwater v. Waterhouse, 52 L. J. Ch. 83.  
(l) R. S.C. Ord. XIX. r. 13.  
(m) Byrne v. Byrne, 5 L. R. Ir. 134; and post, p. 493. But on motion for judgment in default of pleading, in a partition action, some defendants being infants, it was held unnecessary to prove the allegations in the statement of claim, as the usual inquiries would sufficiently protect the infants; Ripley v. Sawyer, 31 C. D. 494. The judges will not now in any case receive evidence upon such a motion; Smith v. Buchan, 36 W. R. 631.

<br>

### --- Page 0608 ---

DEFENCE. 491

against an infant who is almost of age, for default of appearance, the plaintiff being ignorant of the fact of his infancy, the judgment will not be set aside, unless there are good grounds for so doing. An infant was allowed to plead infancy, even after a payment into Court.

But it is often advantageous for an infant to put in a defence. “I think the answer of an infant, stating facts which are intended to form the defence of the infant, extremely important both with regard to the infant and the plaintiff; with regard to the infant, as enabling her to go into evidence as to matters which would not otherwise be in issue in the cause, and with regard to the plaintiff, as apprizing him of the points intended to be set up against him, and showing the case which he is to meet, for the purpose of establishing that which he claims as relief for himself; but whatever admission there may be, or whatever points may be tendered in issue in the answer of the infant defendant, it appears to me, that the plaintiff is not in any degree exonerated from his duty in proving as against the infant the whole case upon which he relies.” A cause has been allowed to stand over, for the defendant to put in a new answer. On attaining twenty-one, the infant is entitled to put in a new defence; but if he take no steps, the old one will be binding upon him by acquiescence.

The proof of infancy lies on the party who pleads it. It may be proved by the entry of the birth in the register.

(n) Furnival v. Brooke, 49 L. T. 184; see ante, pp. 15 (e), 46 (7).

(o) Hitchcock v. Tyson, 2 Esp. 481, n.

(q) Fawkner v. Watts, 1 Atk. 405; and see Savage v. Carroll, 1 Ba. & B. 548.

(r) Bennet v. Lee, 2 Atk. 531.

(s) Cecil v. Salisbury, 2 Vern. 224; Morris v. Morris, 11 Jur. 260.

492

Action on contract entered into by adult and infant.

Admissions by predecessors in title;

ACTIONS AGAINST INFANTS.

the register kept under the provisions of 6 & 7 Will. IV. c. 86 (u). It has been held that the registration is only evidence of the birth having taken place before that date, but not of the exact date of the birth (x). An entry of the date of birth in the parish register of baptisms is not proof (x), even when coupled with an affidavit by the defendant himself (y). Declarations of a deceased father are not evidence (z).

Withdrawal of a plea of infancy, whether true or false, is sufficient consideration for the plaintiff agreeing to accept a smaller amount than he claims (a).

In an action against an adult and an infant on a joint contract, if the latter plead infancy, the plaintiff could not enter a nolle prosequi against him and continue the action against the adult, but had to discontinue his action, and bring a fresh one against the adult only (b). If he sued the adult only, and the latter pleaded in abatement that the promise was joint, the plaintiff might reply a repudiation on the ground of infancy (c); but if the infant appeared and showed that he has not repudiated the contract, the plaintiff would be defeated (d). But pleas in abatement are now abolished (e).

Admissions in the action of the person from whom the infant derives title may be read against him (f). Where, in a suit against an adult heir to establish a will, he admitted the will by answer, and died leaving an infant heir, the latter was held bound by the admission.

(u) See s. 38.

(v) Per Lord Romilly, M.R., Re Wintle, 9 Eq. 878; sed qu.

(x) Wiken v. Law, 8 Stark. 63; R. v. Clapham, 40. & P. 2; Burghart v. Angerstein, 6 C. & P. 690, 696.

(y) Weaver v. Stokes, 1 M. & W. 208.

(z) Figg v. Wedderburne, 6 Jur. 218.

(a) Cooper v. Parker, 14 C. B. 118.

(b) Boyle v. Webster, 17 Q. B. 950; Chandler v. Parkes, 8 Kap. 76; Jaffray v. Frebain, 5 Esp. 47.

(c) Burgess v. Merrill, 4 Taunt. 468.

(d) Gibbs v. Merrill, 8 Taunt. 307.

(e) R. S. C. Ord. XXI. r. 20. But see Kendall v. Hamilton, 4 App. Cas. p. 516.

(f) Holden v. Hearn, 1 Beay. 445.

### --- Page 0610 ---

DEFENCE. 4938

sion, though if the infant heir had been the original defendant, the will must have been proved against him (g). In a creditors’ action for administration, the admission of a debt by the personal representative is sufficient as regards the personal estate (h), but it must be proved against the infant heir (i).

Where there is a deficiency of proof, the Court sometimes directs an inquiry into the facts, and this may be done even against an infant (k); and now in all causes or matters to which any infant is a party, any consent as to the mode of taking evidence or as to any other procedure, if given with the consent of the Court or a judge, by the next friend, guardian, or other person acting on behalf of the person under disability, has the same force and effect as if the infant were under no disability, and had given such consent (l).

Where the infant denies the allegations in the plaintiff’s claim, and questions of fact arise, the action proceeds in the ordinary way (m); but where the defence depends on questions of law, and the facts are not in dispute, the proper course is to verify the statement of claim by affidavit. The action is then placed in the paper, and the infant’s consent to this mode of taking evidence is given, with the consent of the judge, by the guardian ad litem, under the last-mentioned rule (m).

(g) Rotinson v. Cooper, 4 Sim. 181; Lock v. Foote, 4 Sim. 132. It will be remembered that an infant plaintiff is not so bound; Thorns v. Bowyer, 41 L. J. Ch. N. S. 68, ante, p. 478 (uw).

(h) Gascoyne v. Lamb, 11 Jur. 902; Nash v. Benton, Coop. N.C.C. 192.

(i) Hughes v. Eades, 1 Ha. 486.

(k) Quantock v. Bullen, 5 Mad. 81; and see Still v. Hoste, 6 Mad. 192.

(l) R. 8. C. Ord. XVI. r. 21.

(m) Where several defendants, one of whom was an infant, failed to put in any defence, but the infant desired to cross-examine the persons who had made affidavits for the plaintiff, the Court ordered that the action should be set down on notice of trial as against the infant, and on notice of motion for judgment on the statement of claim as against the adult defendants; National Provincial Bank v. Evans, 51 L. J. Ch. 97; see also Ellis v. Robbins, 50 L. J. 513; and Ripley v. Sawyer, cited ante, p. 490 (rm).

(n) Fitzwater v. Waterhouse, 52 L. J. Ch. 83; Gardner v. Tapling, 33 W. R. 473; Wallis v. Wallis, 13 L. R. Ir. 258,

### --- Page 0611 ---

494 ACTIONS

Section 4.—Infant coming in after Judgment.

It sometimes happens that an infant not originally a party to the action, either as being out of the jurisdiction (o), or for some other reason, is afterwards made a party. The evidence already taken cannot in strictness be used against the infant (p); but as it would often only cause useless expense to take it over again, and repeat the proceedings, a reference was commonly directed, whether it would be for the infant’s benefit to adopt the proceedings (q). The proper course for the infant on coming in, is to apply for the appointment of a guardian ad litem by motion, and obtain a separate order for a reference whether it would be for his benefit to adopt the proceedings (r); or the proceedings may be ordered to be adopted, if the judge in chambers should so direct (s).

Where infants are served judgment.

Wherever, in any action for the administration of the estate of a deceased person, or the execution of the trusts of any deed or instrument, or for the partition or sale of any hereditaments, a judgment or an order has been pronounced or made (a) under Order XV.; (b) under Order XXXIII.; or (c) affecting the rights or interests of persons not parties to the action; the Court or a judge may direct that any persons interested in the estate or under the trust or in the hereditaments, shall be served with notice of the judgment or order; and after such notice, such persons shall be bound by the proceedings in the same manner as if they had originally been made parties, and shall be at liberty to attend the proceedings under the judgment or order.

(o) Copley v. Smithson, 5 De G. & S. 583.

(p) Quantock v. Bullen, 5 Mad. 81.

(q) Montgomery v. Reilly, 1 Bl. N. S. 865, 391; Brookfield v. Bradley, Jac. 632; Baillie v. Jackson, 10 Sim. 167.

(r) Copley v. Smithson.

(s) Finch v. Finch, W. N. 1869, 191.

<br>

### --- Page 0612 ---

AGAINST INFANTS.

Any person so served may, within one month after such service, apply to the Court or judge to discharge, vary, or add to the judgment or order (é). It is not necessary for any person served with notice of any judgment or order, to obtain an order for liberty to attend the proceedings, but he is at liberty to attend upon entering an appearance in the central office in the same manner, and subject to the same provisions, as a defendant entering an appearance (uw). Notice of a judgment or order on an infant or person of unsound mind not so found by inquisition is to be served in the same manner as a writ of summons in an action (zx).

At any time during the proceedings at any judge’s chambers under any judgment or order, the judge may, if he thinks fit, require a guardian ad litem to be appointed for any infant, who has been served with notice of such judgment or order (y).

Section 5.—Reviving defective Proceedings.

An action which has been properly instituted may become defective by some change in the interest or liability of some of the parties, as by the birth of a necessary party during the proceedings, or by the death of a party or otherwise. Where any cause or matter has been standing for one year in the cause-book marked as “abated,” or standing over generally, such cause shall at the expiration of the year be struck out of

() RS. C. Ord. XVI. r. 40.

(u) Ibid. r. 41. As to entering an appearance for an infant, see ante, p. 487.

(z) Ibid. r. 44. As to serving a writ on an infant, see ante, p. 487. Notice may be served on infants out of the jurisdiction; Chalmers v. Laurie, 10 Ha. App. 27.

(y) B.S. C. Ord. LV. r. 27; and see Re Smith, 15 P. D. 2.

<br>

### --- Page 0613 ---

Order to carry on proceedings.

Service of Order.

How order may be discharged.

ACTIONS

the cause-book (z). In one case, however, where infants interested in the subject-matter of a suit had nearly attained twenty-one, the Court by consent ordered the cause to be retained in the registrar’s book till they attained twenty-one (a).

Where, by reason of marriage, death, or bankruptcy, or any other event occurring after the commencement of a cause or matter, and causing a change or transmission of interest or liability, or by reason of any person interested coming into existence after the commencement of the cause or matter, it becomes necessary or desirable that any person not already a party should be made a party, or that any person already a party should be made a party in another capacity, an order that the proceedings shall be carried on between the continuing parties, and such new party or parties, may be obtained ex parte on application to the Court or a judge, upon an allegation of such change, or transmission of interest, or liability, or of such person interested having come into existence (6). Such order shall, unless the Court or judge shall otherwise direct, be served upon the continuing party or parties, or their solicitors, and also upon each new party, unless the person making the application be himself the only new party, and the order shall from the time of such service, subject nevertheless to the next two following Rules, be binding on the persons served therewith, and every person served therewith who is not already a party to the cause or matter shall be bound to enter an appearance thereto within the same time and in the same manner as if he had been served with a writ of summons (c). Where any person who is under no disability, or under no disability other than coverture, or being under any disability other than coverture, but

(z) R. S.C. Ord. XVII. r. 10.

(a) Brook v. Todds, 6 Jur. N. 8, 664.  
(6) R. S. C. Ord. XVII. r. 4,

(c) Tbid. r. 5.

<br>

### --- Page 0614 ---

AGAINST INFANTS. 497

having a guardian ad litem in the cause or matter, shall be served with such order as in Rule 4 mentioned, such person may apply to the Court or a judge to discharge or vary such order at any time within twelve days after the service thereof (d). Where any person being under any disability other than coverture, and not having a guardian ad litem in the cause or matter, is served with any order as in Rule 4 mentioned, such person may apply to the Court or a judge to discharge or vary such order at any time within twelve days from the appointment of a guardian ad litem for such party, and until such period of twelve days shall have expired such order shall have no force or effect as against such last-mentioned person (ce).

Under the old practice, an order for revivor could not be made, if any proceedings had been already taken in the suit after the birth of a necessary party, and a supplemental bill had to be filed (f); but where the Chief Clerk’s certificate was filed after the birth of a child, to avoid the expense of a new bill it was allowed to be taken off the file, an order for revivor was then made, and it was replaced on the file (g).

The present practice, where proceedings have been taken after an action has become defective by the birth of an infant who is a necessary party, is for the infant to be made a party by the common order under Rule 4, and the order (h) should go on to direct an inquiry whether any proceedings affecting the infant’s interest have been taken since his birth, and if so, whether it will be fit and proper and for his benefit that he should be bound thereby; and if it be so certified, the infant

(d) R. S. C., Ord. XVII. r. 6.

(ce) Ibid. x. 7.

(f) Capps v. Capps, 4 Ch. 1; Auster v. Haines, ibid., 445; Egremont v. Thomson, ibid., 448; Scott v. Duncombe, 9 Eq. 665; Powell v. Phillips, 19 W. R. 562; Askew v. Rooth, W. N. 1875, 9. See also Garrett v. Jancefield, 11 W. R. 869; Hilyard v. Field, 20 W. R. 198.

(g) Cuthbert v. Harmby, 18 Eq. 202.

(h) See Seton, 1527, Form 3.

### --- Page 0615 ---

498 ACTIONS

is to be bound accordingly. If such inquiry be answered in the negative, the plaintiff or person having the conduct can still proceed by supplemental action (j). Where the plaintiffs refused to add infants born during the proceedings, an order was made under Rule 7 on the application of the defendants, and (though accounting parties) they were given the conduct of the proceedings (i).

Interest of administrator during minoritate expiring. Where a creditor filed a bill for administration against an administrator during minoritate, and the infant on attaining twenty-one neglected to take out probate, the plaintiff was allowed to revive the suit by an order making the late infant a defendant (j). For other changes of interest, &c., where an order will be made under the rule, see Morg. Chanc. Ord., 6th ed., 350, and the Annual Practice, 1889–90, 355–363.

Section 6.—Costs of Infant Defendant.

Charged on property: An infant may be made to pay the costs of an action where he has been guilty of fraud (m); or if there is property of the infant, with which the Court can deal, the costs of an unsuccessful defence may be charged on it (n). Costs of an infant defendant in a suit for administering or managing his property, may be charged upon it (o). A guardian ad litem is not liable for costs.

(i) Peter v. Thomas-Peter, 26 C.D. 181; see form of Order, Burney’s Forms, p. 88.

(k) Wicks v. Wicks, W.N. 1887, 15.

(l) Sewell v. Ransfurd, 21 W.R. 244; see Ke Gould, 61 L.T. 417.

(m) Chubb v. Griffith, 35 Beav. 127.

(n) Orford v. Churchill, 3 V. & B. 59.

(o) Mandeno v. Mandeno, Kay, App. 2. See Exp. Davies, 16 Jur. 882.

### --- Page 0616 ---

AGAINST INFANTS. 499

merely because he raises an unsuccessful defence. In Kilsey v. Cox, where the assignee in bankruptcy set aside a voluntary settlement on the wife and child of the settlor, the Court said all it could do for the infant was not to make him pay costs.

Where the Court or a judge appoints one of the solicitors of the Court to be guardian ad litem of an infant, the Court or judge may direct that the costs to be incurred in the performance of the duties of such office shall be borne and paid, either by the parties or some one or more of the parties to the cause or matter in which such appointment is made, or out of any fund in Court in which such infant may be interested, and may give directions for the repayment or allowance of such costs, as the justice and circumstances of the case may require.

Where the solicitor to the suitors’ fee fund is appointed, it is the settled rule that the plaintiff pays the costs, and adds them to his own; there is no jurisdiction to order payment out of the suitors’ fund. This is the practice in a foreclosure action, even where the security is insufficient. In a partition action all costs up to the hearing are in the discretion of the Court; and the practice is that the entire costs, in the case of a sale, are paid out of the proceeds; and, in the case of a partition, are in ordinary cases borne by the parties.

(y) 26 Beay, 95.

(s) RLS. C., Ord. LXV. r. 13; and see Ord. XXXIII. r. 9.

(1) Frazer v. Thompson, 4 De G. & J. 659, 662.

(x) Newbery v. Martin, 15 Jur. 166; Robey v. Whitewood, cited in Robinson v. Aston, 9 Sur. 224.

(x) Harris v. Hamlyn, 3 De G. & S. 470.

(y) Partition Act, 1863, s. 10.

### --- Page 0618 ---

CHAPTER XXXI.  
HEARING AND JUDGMENT.  

Section 1.—When Cause heard in private.  

WHERE wards of Court are concerned, the Court will exercise its own discretion as to hearing a cause or matter in private, whether the other party consents or not (a).  

Section 2.—How far Infant bound by Judgment (b).  

(1) Infant plaintiff.  

The Court has no authority to give an infant a power of alienation, even for his own benefit (c), but this must not now be considered as applying to cases of election (d). Subject to this, an infant plaintiff is, as a general rule, bound by a judgment or order in his own action as much as an adult (e), even though made by consent without any reference whether it is for the infant’s benefit (f), or though there has been some

(b) See Seton, 712.  

(c) Simson v. Jones, 2 R. & M. 374; Taylor v. Phillips, 1 Ves. sen. 229; 2 ib. 23.  

(d) See post, sec. 6.  

(e) Sheffield v. Buckingham, 1 Atk. 681; Gregory v. Molesworth, 3 Atk. 626.  

(f) Wall v. Bushby, 1 B.C.C. 484.
